Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Personification & Imagery

Personification gives human qualities to non-human things. Imagery uses vivid descriptions that appeal to the five senses (sight, sound, touch, taste, smell).

Example: Personification: The wind whispered through the trees.
Imagery: The cold stone floor sent shivers up through his bare feet.

Question 1:

Identify the personification or imagery in each sentence and explain which sense it appeals to:

1. The old house groaned under the weight of the storm. 2. The bread was warm and golden, filling the room with the smell of honey and wheat. 3. Shadows danced across the castle walls.

0 / 10 words
defiance

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. forethought

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 165).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Prometheus was a Titan who thought ahead. His name meant exactly that — Forethought — and it was accurate. He had sided with Zeus in the great war not because he was told to but because he had thought it through and decided it was right. Zeus trusted him for it.

But Prometheus cared more about human beings than any other god did. He watched them from Olympus — small figures huddling in darkness when the sun went down, eating their food raw, shivering through winters with nothing to warm them. He could see what they were capable of and what they were being prevented from achieving by one simple absence.

Fire.

He went to Zeus and asked that humans be given fire. It was a reasonable request, he thought. He had arguments prepared.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire

Zeus said no. Fire was divine. It belonged to the gods. Humans weren't ready for it. The conversation ended there, the way conversations with Zeus tended to end.

Prometheus went home and thought about this.

He traveled to Lemnos, where Hephaestus kept his forge, and found a hollow fennel stalk — the kind that burns slowly from the inside, carrying an ember long distances without going out. He lit it from the divine fire of the forge. He carried it, glowing and hidden, all the way back to earth.

He gave fire to the humans.

The results were everything he had hoped. People lit their hearths and cooked their food. They sat together in warmth and light and told stories. They fired clay into pots, smelted metal into tools, used torches to extend the working day. They built better shelter, developed crafts and music and art. Fire did not just warm them — it changed what they could become.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire

Zeus looked down from Olympus and saw fires flickering across the face of the earth like tiny reflected stars.

He was not pleased.

The punishment Zeus chose was not quick. Quick would have been almost merciful. He wanted something that would last: Prometheus chained to a mountain in the Caucasus, immovable and inescapable. Each day an eagle came and ate his liver. Each night the liver grew back. Each morning the eagle returned.

This went on for a very long time.

Heracles eventually freed him — killed the eagle, broke the chains. But for the long years before that, Prometheus was on his mountain.

He did not say he regretted it. Every storyteller who told this story was careful to note that. The eagle came, and the liver grew back, and the eagle came again, and Prometheus on his mountain looked down at the earth below where the fires still burned, where they would never go out now, where humans sat in warmth telling each other stories.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire

He had done what he believed was right. He had known the cost. He paid it.

The fire stayed.

This is why, when you strike a match or sit beside a fire on a cold night, you are holding something stolen from the gods by someone who decided the price was worth it.

The Greeks thought you should know that.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Prometheus knew what the punishment would be before he stole the fire, and he did it anyway. Do you think he was brave, reckless, or something else? Can you think of anyone in history — or in your own life — who accepted a serious personal cost to help others?

Page 15

🎉 Day 101 Complete!

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire

Words

Definitions

A vocabulary word
A vocabulary word
A vocabulary word
A rule that forbids something — a clear instruction not to do a specific thing.
Certain to happen — impossible to avoid no matter what you do.
Continuing to work at something difficult without giving up, even over a long time.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Review your words

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Pandoras Box

Pandora's Box

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

After Prometheus gave fire to humanity, Zeus decided to send a gift of his own.

He went to Hephaestus and asked him to make a woman out of clay — the first mortal woman, shaped by divine hands. Then he invited every god on Olympus to contribute something to her.

Aphrodite gave her beauty. Athena gave her skill in weaving and the arts. Hermes gave her curiosity and a persuasive voice. Apollo gave her music. The Graces gave her charm. Hera gave her perseverance. The Seasons dressed her in flowers.

Zeus named her Pandora — all-gifted — and gave her one more thing: a large sealed jar with very clear instructions attached to it.

Do not open this.

Then he sent her to earth, to be the wife of Epimetheus. Epimetheus was the brother of Prometheus, and his name meant Afterthought — because unlike his brother, who understood the future, Epimetheus understood things only after they had already happened. Prometheus had warned him: never accept a gift from Zeus.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Pandoras Box

Epimetheus forgot this warning the moment he saw Pandora, which is perhaps the most Afterthought thing he ever did.

They were happy together. The jar sat in the corner of their house, sealed, waiting.

The problem was Hermes's gift. Curiosity is wonderful — it drives invention and discovery and the desire to understand — but it does not combine well with a sealed jar you've been told never to open. The jar was there every day. Every day Pandora walked past it. Every day the wondering grew a little larger and a little harder to ignore.

What was inside? Why couldn't she open it? Zeus had given it to her — surely it was hers in some sense. Surely she had a right to know what she owned.

One day she opened it.

What came out was everything difficult about human life — all the things the world had not yet known: sickness, sorrow, envy, cruelty, conflict, old age, disappointment, grief, all the hard and painful things that make a human life difficult. They had been locked in the jar, and now they poured out like smoke, spreading into the world, into the air, into the future of every person who would ever live.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Pandoras Box

Pandora slammed the lid down.

In the bottom of the jar, still inside, was one thing that had not escaped.

Hope.

The Greeks argued about this for centuries, the way they argued about everything worth arguing about. Was hope left behind because Pandora caught it in time — something preserved for humanity in the disaster? Or was hope itself a kind of trap — the thing that keeps you suffering when you might otherwise give up? Was it a gift or one more complication?

They never fully agreed.

What they did agree on is this: Pandora was not evil. She was curious, which is one of the best things a person can be. She had been given curiosity as a gift, by a god who knew exactly what curiosity would do with a sealed jar and a prohibition. The question of who was really responsible for what got loose in the world — the woman who opened the jar, or the god who made the jar and put her within reach of it — is a question the Greeks considered worth asking.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Pandoras Box

They were, in this way, honest about their gods.

The jar is empty now. Everything that was in it is already out in the world.

But hope is still there, in the bottom, if you look for it.

Page 10

✍️ Comprehension Questions

Show what you understood

Page 11

🎨 Language Skills

Week 33 Focus: Greek/Latin Roots in English

📚 What You're Learning:

Many English words come from Greek roots

✨ Examples:

HYDRO (water): dehydrate, hydroelectric

CHRON (time): chronic, chronological

PHOTO (light): photograph, photosynthesis

🎯 Your Turn:

1. What does 'mythology' mean if MYTH=story and LOGY=study?

Definitions

A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
To take someone away by force, against their will.
An agreement where both sides give up something to reach a solution neither would have fully chosen.
To become less strict or harsh — to give in after a period of refusing.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Review your words

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Persephone and the Seasons

Persephone and the Seasons

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Demeter was the goddess of growing things — grain and harvest and the patient work of the earth — and she had one daughter named Persephone who was the whole world to her.

Persephone was joyful and curious, a young goddess who moved through meadows like spring itself, flowers turning toward her as she passed. She spent her days gathering flowers with the nymphs, running through tall grass, as glad to be alive as a creature can be.

One afternoon she found a narcissus she had never seen before — a hundred blossoms on a single stem, its perfume drifting across the whole meadow. She reached for it.

The earth opened.

From the crack in the ground rose Hades, lord of the underworld, in a chariot of black horses. He had been watching Persephone. He had gone to Zeus and asked permission to make her his queen. Zeus — who was Persephone's father, though he was better at ruling the sky than paying attention to his children — had said yes.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Persephone and the Seasons

He had not mentioned this to Demeter.

Before Persephone could cry out, the earth closed over her.

The meadow was empty. The nymphs had seen nothing.

Demeter searched for nine days and nine nights without stopping — no food, no sleep, a torch in each hand, calling her daughter's name across every hillside and shore. The other gods would not tell her what had happened. The earth would not answer. She was entirely alone in her grief, which is the worst kind.

On the tenth day she found Hecate, who had heard a scream, and Helios, the sun god, who sees everything that happens on earth from his chariot. Helios told her the truth: Hades had taken Persephone, and Zeus had known.

Demeter did not rage. She did something quieter and more absolute. She left Olympus. She walked the earth disguised as an old woman and refused to do her work. She was the goddess of the harvest, and nothing would grow while her daughter was gone. The fields dried and cracked. The orchards stopped bearing fruit. The grain didn't rise. Animals had nothing to eat. People had nothing to eat.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Persephone and the Seasons

The earth was dying.

Zeus sent the gods to Demeter one by one, asking her to return. She said one thing: bring back my daughter.

Zeus finally sent Hermes to the underworld to retrieve Persephone. Hades agreed, with a quiet smile and the manner of someone who has already arranged the ending he wanted.

Before Persephone left, she ate six pomegranate seeds. Whether she was tricked, or hungry, or understood what she was doing — the stories don't quite agree. But the rule of the underworld was clear: anyone who ate its food belonged to it.

She could not stay on earth. She could not stay entirely below.

The compromise that was reached — and it was a compromise, which means no one got everything they wanted — was this: Persephone would spend part of each year below with Hades, and part of each year above with her mother.

When Persephone returns from the underworld, the earth bursts into spring. Demeter's joy is visible in every flower, every green shoot, every warm morning after a long winter. When Persephone must go back down, Demeter lets the world go cold. She does not abandon it entirely — the work must continue, the cycle must turn — but she grieves, and the earth grieves with her.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Persephone and the Seasons

Every autumn, the world goes golden and then bare. Every spring, it comes back.

The Greeks looked at the seasons and saw a mother's love and a mother's grief, cycling through the year like breathing in and breathing out, and they thought: yes. That is exactly what it feels like.

That is exactly right.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Zeus gave permission for Persephone to be taken without telling Demeter. He was Persephone's father. What does his choice tell you about him? How do you think a parent should balance their own decisions with the wellbeing of their child?

Page 15

🎉 Day 104 Complete!

Persephone and the Seasons

Words

defiance
penance
prohibition
inevitable
perseverance
abduct
compromise
relent

Definitions

A rule that forbids something — a clear instruction not to do a specific thing.
To take someone away by force, against their will.
An agreement where both sides give up something to reach a solution neither would have fully chosen.
To become less strict or harsh — to give in after a period of refusing.
Certain to happen — impossible to avoid no matter what you do.
Look up the definition of penance
Look up the definition of forethought
Look up the definition of defiance
Continuing to work at something difficult without giving up, even over a long time.

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Point of View

First person (I, me, we) — the narrator is a character in the story.
Third person limited — the narrator knows one character's thoughts.
Third person omniscient — the narrator knows everyone's thoughts.

Example: First: "I could feel the fear rising in my chest."
Third limited: "Victor felt the fear rising in his chest."
Third omniscient: "Victor felt afraid, while the creature felt hopeful."

Question 1:

What point of view is this week\'s reading written in? Give a specific example from the text that proves it.

0 / 10 words
methodical

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. hubris

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 170).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Icarus and the Wings of Wax

Icarus and the Wings of Wax

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Daedalus had built the Labyrinth.

He was the greatest inventor the ancient world had ever seen — there was nothing he could not design, nothing he could not build. The Labyrinth on Crete was his masterpiece: a maze so complex, so perfectly constructed that nothing that entered it could find its way out. King Minos had wanted exactly that, and Daedalus had delivered it completely.

The problem was that Daedalus now knew too much. King Minos couldn't let the architect of his secrets simply leave the island. He had Daedalus and his son Icarus locked in a tower on the cliffs above the sea.

Daedalus looked at the ocean that surrounded them. No boat would leave without Minos knowing. He looked up at the birds that rode the sea winds past the cliff top. He thought the way inventors think — systematically, without ruling anything out too quickly.

He began collecting feathers. Every feather that drifted down from the sea winds, every bird that could be convinced to contribute — he kept them all, sorted them by size, and spent months arranging them in careful layers into the shapes of wings. He used beeswax to bind feathers to each other and to frames of flexible wood. He shaped two sets: large ones for himself, a smaller pair for Icarus.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Icarus and the Wings of Wax

He called his son over and explained everything. He was careful. He was thorough. He was an engineer explaining a piece of equipment to the person who would trust their life to it.

"Middle height," he said. "Not too low — the spray from the sea will wet the feathers and weigh you down. Not too high — the heat of the sun will melt the wax and the wings will come apart. Middle height. Steady. You understand?"

Icarus said he understood.

They launched from the cliff top on a morning with good winds.

What happened next, Daedalus understood exactly, and Icarus understood not at all — the difference between knowing something and knowing it.

Icarus had never been in the air before. He had never felt the world drop away beneath him. He had never seen the island of Crete shrink to something he could have covered with his hand, or felt the sun at that angle, warmer and more golden and more present than it ever felt from the ground. He had never moved through space like a bird, like something that belonged in the sky, like something free.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Icarus and the Wings of Wax

He flew higher.

His father called to him — the words torn apart by wind, arriving as fragments of sound that Icarus knew were the words he'd already been told. He knew what his father was saying. He knew it. But the sky above him was extraordinary and the sun was extraordinary and he was sixteen years old and no one had ever done this before, and from up here the height felt like power rather than danger.

The wax began to soften. Gradually at first. Then a feather came loose. Then two. Then a section of the wing's edge peeled away in the wind, spinning downward.

He had time to understand what was happening. That much the Greeks gave him — a moment of clarity before the end, the full knowledge of what the height had cost him.

Daedalus heard his son call out. He turned in time to see the wings come apart, the feathers scattering across the sky in a white shower, Icarus falling toward the blue water far below.

He circled the spot for a long time.

Then he flew on alone to Sicily, because he had to land somewhere, and there was nowhere else to go.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Icarus and the Wings of Wax

The sea where Icarus fell is called the Icarian Sea. It always will be.

The Greeks did not tell this story as a simple warning about disobedience. They told it as something more complicated — about a father who built the best thing he knew how to build and still couldn't make it enough. About a boy who had been told exactly what would happen and felt the sky and understood, too late, the difference between a rule and a wall.

About how some experiences cannot be described to someone who hasn't had them, only felt — and how, by the time you feel them, the instruction manual is already miles below you.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Daedalus explained everything: middle height, not too low, not too high. Icarus understood, and flew higher anyway. Do you think Icarus made a choice, or was his behavior something almost inevitable given who he was and what he was experiencing? Is there a difference?

Page 14

🎉 Day 106 Complete!

Icarus and the Wings of Wax

revoke

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (2 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 2

Words

1. hubris
1. avarice

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 170).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 170).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Midas and the Golden Touch

Midas and the Golden Touch

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

King Midas was not a bad man.

He was friendly and generous and well-liked by his people, and his one weakness — a love of gold, its color and weight and the way it caught light — was, as weaknesses go, not the most dangerous kind. He had a treasury full of it and was always happy to see more.

One morning his servants brought him an old man they'd found asleep in the royal garden. He was peculiar-looking — round red face, vine leaves tangled in his hair, the unmistakable air of someone who had been having an excellent party somewhere and wandered off. They'd tied him up, not knowing what else to do.

Midas looked at him and recognized him immediately: Silenus, the companion and old tutor of Dionysus, the god of wine and celebration. He freed the old man at once, drew him a warm bath, had a proper meal prepared, and entertained him cheerfully for ten days before sending him safely back to Dionysus.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Midas and the Golden Touch

Dionysus was delighted. He offered Midas any wish.

Midas didn't have to think. "I wish that everything I touch would turn to gold."

Dionysus looked at him. He was a god of joy, and he liked Midas, and he had offered the wish freely. He granted it. But the expression on his face when he did it was the expression of someone who already knows how a story ends.

Midas went home full of excitement. He touched a twig — gold. A stone — gold. He plucked a flower and held a small, perfect, heavy golden rose, every petal preserved exactly.

He went to sit down to dinner.

He lifted his bread. It turned to gold in his hand, hard and cold and impossible to eat. He lifted his wine cup — gold. He reached for an olive — gold. He was sitting at a table covered in golden food he could not touch, in a golden chair that had stopped being a chair, in a palace that was rapidly becoming a very expensive problem.

He was starving.

His daughter came to comfort him when she saw his face.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Midas and the Golden Touch

She turned to gold.

She stood there — perfectly herself in every feature, her arms still extended toward him, her expression still full of concern — cold and solid and golden and gone.

Midas left the palace and found Dionysus and didn't speak for a moment, because what do you say.

"Take it back," he said. "Please. Take it back."

Dionysus told him to go to the source of the river Pactolus in Lydia and wash his hands in it. Midas ran. He washed his hands in the headwaters and felt the power drain out of him and flow into the river — which is why, the Greeks said, the river Pactolus ran with gold dust for centuries afterward, washing out of the hills where Midas had washed away his wish.

He went home. His daughter was herself again. He held her for a long time.

After that, Midas lost interest in gold entirely. He gave most of his treasury away. He took to wandering in the hills and forests, which suited him much better. He became a devoted follower of Pan, the wild god of the woods, and spent his days listening to the wind in the reeds and watching the animals move through the trees, none of which was gold, all of which was exactly what it was.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Midas and the Golden Touch

He was the richest man who ever lived for about four hours. He was much happier after he stopped being that.

The Greeks told the story of Midas not as a warning against greed exactly — Midas wasn't greedy in a cruel way, just enthusiastic about something in a direction that happened to be wrong. They told it as a reminder that getting what you think you want has consequences you cannot fully imagine in the moment of wanting it.

They also pointed out that his daughter came back.

The gold in the river stayed. That part didn't undo itself.

Be specific about what you ask for, and think it through first. Dionysus will not always be in a reversing mood.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Midas asked for the golden touch immediately, without thinking it through. Dionysus warned him with an expression — he didn't say anything, but his face showed he knew what was coming. Why do you think the Greeks gave Dionysus that knowing look? What responsibility do we have when we can see someone is about to make a terrible mistake?

Page 14

🎉 Day 107 Complete!

Midas and the Golden Touch

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Midas and the Golden Touch
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced language skills
  • ✅ Completed writing practice

Lesson 109

5th Grade ELA

Greek Mythology

Perseus and Medusa

Page 2

Today you will:

  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

petrify

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (3 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 3

Words

1. hubris
1. avarice
1. petrify

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 170).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 170).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 175).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Perseus and Medusa

Perseus and Medusa

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Perseus grew up poor on a small island with his mother Danaë, and he grew up brave, which was worth considerably more.

The king of the island, Polydectes, wanted to marry Danaë. Danaë did not want to marry Polydectes. Perseus was the obstacle — as long as he was there to protect her, Polydectes couldn't simply force the matter.

Polydectes announced a great feast and made a show of asking every guest to contribute a horse as a gift for a bride he was supposedly pursuing elsewhere. Perseus, who had no horse and no money, said rashly that he would bring whatever was asked — even the head of Medusa.

Polydectes smiled the smile of a man whose plan has just worked. "Then bring me the head of Medusa."

Medusa was a Gorgon — snakes for hair, bronze hands, golden wings, a gaze that turned anything living instantly to stone. She lived at the edge of the world, which was not a place most people returned from.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Perseus and Medusa

Perseus left the island with no plan and almost no idea what he was doing. Two gods decided this was their moment.

Athena arrived first. She had her own complicated reasons for wanting Medusa dealt with. She gave Perseus a shield polished like a mirror and told him to look only at the reflection — never directly. She was crisp and practical about it, like someone who has thought through a problem and is offering the solution.

Hermes came next, quick and cheerful. He gave Perseus directions to the Graeae — three ancient women who shared one eye and one tooth between them, passing them back and forth as needed. They were the only ones who knew the way to the Hesperides, who had the tools he needed.

Perseus found the Graeae bickering in the dark. He waited until the eye was being passed between them — one holding it out, the next not yet holding it — and grabbed it.

Three ancient women, suddenly blind, suddenly very interested in negotiating.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Perseus and Medusa

He got the directions.

The Hesperides gave him a kibisis — a magical bag — and winged sandals and a cap of invisibility. Fully equipped, he flew to the Gorgons' lair.

He kept his eyes on the shield. In the reflection — reversed, flat, strange but safe — he could see Medusa sleeping among her sisters. He moved carefully, watching only the mirror image, and struck.

He had her head in the bag before the other Gorgons woke.

He flew home by a different route and found, over the coast of Ethiopia, a young woman chained to a cliff above the sea, a sea monster moving through the water below her. Her name was Andromeda, and she had been offered as a sacrifice to appease the monster.

Perseus killed the monster. He asked Andromeda's father for her hand in marriage. The father agreed enthusiastically, then tried to take it back when a previous suitor showed up with an army.

Perseus took Medusa's head out of the bag.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Perseus and Medusa

The army became a very crowded stone sculpture garden.

He went home to Seriphos. Polydectes had been terrorizing his mother in his absence. Perseus found the king and his court together.

He took out the head again.

He gave the head to Athena, who set it in the center of her shield, where it remained — turning enemies to stone whenever she faced them in battle. A practical gift that she used well.

Perseus went on to become a king and to have a long life, which is not as common in Greek mythology as it should be. He never forgot the mirror trick — the idea that sometimes the only way to face something that will destroy you if you look at it directly is to find an angle, an approach, a way of seeing it sideways.

The Greeks told that to their children for a reason.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Perseus couldn't look at Medusa directly — he had to use the shield as a mirror. The Greeks used this image as a metaphor for facing terrible things: sometimes the only way to look at something dangerous is sideways. What do you think that means? Can you think of a situation where a direct approach might make things worse?

Page 14

🎉 Day 109 Complete!

Perseus and Medusa

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Perseus and Medusa
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed journal reflection

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Prefixes and Suffixes

Prefixes are added to the beginning of words to change meaning (un-, re-, pre-, dis-, mis-, over-).
Suffixes are added to the end (-tion, -ly, -ful, -less, -ment, -ness, -able).

Example: unforgivable = not + able to be forgiven
carelessness = without care + state of being

Question 1:

Break each word into prefix/root/suffix and define each part:

1. unbreakable 2. misjudgment 3. overconfident

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. penance

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 175).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Heracles and the Twelve Labors

Heracles and the Twelve Labors

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Heracles was the strongest man who ever lived. His father was Zeus, and the divine strength ran through him like current through wire — always present, always dangerous, capable of anything.

Hera hated him for the simple fact of his existence and made his life difficult from birth. When he was eight months old, she sent two enormous serpents into his crib. They found him already awake. He strangled them both and was found in the morning playing with the bodies, which tells you everything you need to know about Heracles as an infant.

He grew up and married and was genuinely happy — which was when Hera sent the madness. A cloud of confusion so total that he did not know himself or anyone around him. In that terrible fog, he caused harm to his own household. When the madness lifted and he understood what he had done, it broke something in him.

He went to the Oracle at Delphi and asked what he must do. The Oracle sent him to his cousin King Eurystheus: twelve years of service, twelve labors, whatever was commanded.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Heracles and the Twelve Labors

Eurystheus was a coward who was afraid of Heracles and designed the labors to be impossible. He was wrong about what impossible meant.

The first labor: the Nemean Lion, a creature whose skin no weapon could pierce. Heracles figured this out quickly, dropped his weapons, and wrestled it to death with his bare hands. He used its own claws to skin it and wore the hide as armor forever after.

The second: the Lernaean Hydra, which grew two heads for every one cut off. Heracles brought his nephew Iolaus, who cauterized each stump with a torch as Heracles cut. It took both of them. Heracles was not too proud to admit that.

The third: the Ceryneian Hind, a golden-horned deer sacred to Artemis. He could not harm it. He chased it for a full year until it tired enough to catch, then carried it back alive and released it unharmed after showing it to Eurystheus, who had not specified that catching it would be sufficient but discovered it was going to have to be.

The fourth: the Erymanthian Boar. Captured.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Heracles and the Twelve Labors

The fifth: the Augean Stables — three thousand cattle, thirty years of accumulation, clean it in a day. Heracles looked at the stables and then at the two rivers nearby and diverted both of them to flow through. Done before sunset.

The sixth: the Stymphalian Birds, man-eating birds with bronze feathers and beaks. He borrowed bronze castanets from Athena, crashed them together to startle the birds into the air, and shot them with arrows as they rose.

The seventh through twelfth: a fire-breathing bull, the man-eating mares of Diomedes, the belt of Hippolyta queen of the Amazons, the cattle of Geryon at the edge of the world, the golden apples of the Hesperides guarded by an unsleeping dragon, and finally the three-headed dog Cerberus himself — dragged up from the underworld alive, shown to Eurystheus, and returned.

Eurystheus received news of each completed labor in a large bronze jar, which he hid inside when Heracles came to report, because Eurystheus had been terrified of Heracles from the beginning and twelve successful impossibilities had not improved the situation.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Heracles and the Twelve Labors

The twelve labors were complete. Heracles served his penance. The debt, as the Oracle had defined it, was paid.

He went on to other adventures — there were always more adventures — and eventually, much later, was killed by a poisoned shirt sent by someone who thought it was a love charm. He died in agony on a funeral pyre he built himself.

Zeus took him to Olympus. He became a god. He was reconciled, finally, with Hera, who gave him her daughter Hebe as wife.

The Greeks told the twelve labors to show what a human being could do when pushed past every limit — not because they were easy or because Heracles was a god (he was half, which mattered), but because he kept going. The labors were designed to be impossible. He refused to agree.

The Augean Stables took a day.

The rivers are still there.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The madness Hera sent was the cause of everything — Heracles didn't choose what he did in that fog. But he still accepted responsibility and did the twelve labors to make amends. Do you think he had to? Is it possible to owe penance for something you did while you didn't know yourself?

Page 14

🎉 Day 111 Complete!

Heracles and the Twelve Labors

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Heracles and the Twelve Labors
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed journal reflection

  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

expedition

oath

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (2 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 2

Words

1. penance
1. expedition

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 175).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 175).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Jason and the Golden Fleece

Jason and the Golden Fleece

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Jason had a rightful claim to the throne of Iolcos. His father had been king before his uncle Pelias had stolen it while Jason was too young to stop him. He grew up in the wilderness, trained by the centaur Chiron — the wisest teacher in Greece — and came back when he was old enough to claim what was his.

He arrived in the city having lost one sandal crossing a flooded river, which fulfilled a prophecy Pelias had been dreading about a one-sandaled man. Pelias looked at Jason's single sandal and smiled the smile of someone thinking quickly.

"Of course the throne is yours," he said warmly. "But first — do something worthy of a king. Bring me the Golden Fleece, from Colchis at the far edge of the Black Sea, guarded by a dragon that never sleeps. Do that, and the throne is yours."

He expected Jason to fail. He expected Jason to die. He had calculated both outcomes as acceptable.

Jason had a ship built — the Argo, the finest ship Greece had ever seen — and sent out a call for the greatest heroes of the age. They came. Heracles. Orpheus. Atalanta. The twin heroes Castor and Pollux. The seer Mopsus. Fifty of them in total, sailing on the Argo: the Argonauts.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Jason and the Golden Fleece

The journey was extraordinary and nearly fatal several times. The Clashing Rocks — two enormous cliffs that crashed together to destroy anything that tried to pass — tested them first. They sent a dove through to time the rocks; it lost only a tail feather. The Argonauts rowed through as the rocks were separating, nearly lost the stern, and were through.

At Colchis, the king Aeetes had no intention of giving up the Fleece. He set an impossible task: plow a field with two fire-breathing bronze bulls, sow it with dragon's teeth, defeat the army of warriors that would spring from the teeth, all in one day. He had set this test before. No one had survived it.

Then he made a mistake: he let Jason come to his palace.

Medea was the king's daughter, a powerful sorceress, and she saw Jason and made the kind of decision you can't unmake. She was throwing away her entire life and she knew it, and she did it anyway. She sent for Jason secretly and told him exactly what to do: a fireproof ointment for the bulls, a stone thrown into the center of the tooth-warriors to make them fight each other, her herbs to put the dragon to sleep.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Jason and the Golden Fleece

In exchange: take me with you.

Jason swore by the gods he would honor her. He did the tasks, got the Fleece, and they fled Colchis in the night with Aeetes's fleet pursuing them.

They made it home. Pelias was presented with the Golden Fleece and discovered, when he went to examine it, that it did not contain any clauses about actually giving up the throne. He had no intention of honoring a promise he'd made to someone he'd expected to be dead.

Medea handled this. She handled it in a way that resolved the problem permanently. Jason became king of Iolcos.

He was not, in the end, worthy of Medea. This is the uncomfortable center of the story that the Greeks told honestly: he later set her aside to marry a princess, reasoning that it was a politically advantageous match. He had forgotten — or chosen to forget — what she had given up, what she had done, the oath he had sworn by the gods.

The gods did not forget.

The Argo — the ship that had carried the greatest heroes of the age on the greatest voyage of the age — rotted on the beach in Corinth. One day its stern post fell and struck Jason, who was sleeping beneath it. He died under the wreckage of his own glory.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Jason and the Golden Fleece

The Greeks told Jason's story in full, without editing out the parts where their hero was small. The Golden Fleece was real. The oath was real. Both things could be true at once, and the story insisted you hold them together.

You can do the remarkable thing and still fail the test that matters most.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Medea gave up everything — her family, her home, her country — because she loved Jason and he swore an oath to her. He later broke that oath for political advantage. The gods noticed. What does the story say about the weight of a promise — especially one sworn by something larger than yourself?

Page 14

🎉 Day 112 Complete!

Jason and the Golden Fleece

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Jason and the Golden Fleece
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced language skills
  • ✅ Completed writing practice

  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

cunning

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

hospitality

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
hubris

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (3 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 3

Words

1. penance
1. expedition
1. cunning

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 175).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 175).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 180).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Odysseus and the Cyclops

Odysseus and the Cyclops

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

The war at Troy lasted ten years. Getting home took Odysseus ten more.

This is one of the things that happened on the way.

Odysseus and his crew stopped at an island that looked peaceful from the water — green hills, smoke rising from inland, the sound of sheep. Odysseus took one ship and twelve men to investigate. They found a cave: enormous pens of sheep inside, great wheels of cheese stacked against the walls, pails of milk in rows. Everything was built for someone much larger than a person.

Several of his men said, quite reasonably, that they should take some cheese and leave immediately.

Odysseus wanted to see who lived there. He was curious — one of his greatest qualities and, regularly, one of his most expensive ones.

They waited.

The cave's owner came home at dark. He was a Cyclops — a giant with one eye in the center of his forehead, named Polyphemus. He rolled an enormous boulder across the cave entrance, lit a fire, and then noticed the men in the corner.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Odysseus and the Cyclops

"Who are you?"

Odysseus stepped forward and invoked the laws of hospitality — the sacred obligation of hosts to guests, protected by Zeus himself. He was polite about it. He made a reasonable case.

Polyphemus ate two of his men and went to sleep.

Odysseus wanted desperately to draw his sword. He stopped himself: the boulder across the entrance was too large for all twelve remaining men to move. If he killed the Cyclops, they were sealed in the cave with a dead giant, which solved nothing.

He needed the giant alive and willing to move the boulder. He needed a plan.

In the morning, after Polyphemus ate two more men and left with his sheep, Odysseus found a large wooden club in the cave and spent the day sharpening one end to a point and hardening it in the fire's embers. Then he waited.

When Polyphemus returned and asked if he wanted anything, Odysseus offered him wine — strong wine, unmixed with water. The Cyclops, who had never had wine, drank a bowl. Then another. Then asked for more and asked his guest's name.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Odysseus and the Cyclops

"Nobody," Odysseus said. "My name is Nobody."

Polyphemus, drunk and pleased, said he would eat Nobody last as a gift. Then he fell asleep.

Odysseus drove the sharpened stake into the fire until the tip glowed. He and his men drove it into the sleeping Cyclops's single eye.

Polyphemus screamed and the other Cyclopes came running to the cave entrance, calling through the boulder: "Who hurt you? Who's hurting you?"

"Nobody!" Polyphemus roared. "Nobody is killing me!"

A pause from outside. "Well, if nobody is hurting you, you must be sick. Pray to your father Poseidon."

They left.

Polyphemus tore the boulder away to let out his sheep in the morning, feeling each one as it passed to make sure no man rode out. Odysseus tied his men under the bellies of the sheep, himself beneath the largest ram. They passed through the giant's hands one by one.

Outside. Running for the ship.

They were almost to the water when Odysseus did something that cost him ten years: he shouted back at Polyphemus. He couldn't help it. He had won. He wanted the Cyclops to know who had won.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Odysseus and the Cyclops

"You want to know who blinded you? It was Odysseus, son of Laertes, from Ithaca!"

Polyphemus was the son of Poseidon. He prayed to his father then — pray that Odysseus never gets home, or if he must, that he arrives late and alone and finds only grief.

Poseidon, god of the sea, heard the prayer.

Odysseus got home ten years later. Alone. To grief.

He had been brilliant, resourceful, controlled, perfect — right up until the moment his pride spoke before he could stop it. The Greeks noted this carefully and told it to their children: the same quality that makes you clever enough to win can make you careless enough to give back everything you won.

Nobody defeated the Cyclops.

Odysseus lost the war with his own mouth.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Nobody was a perfect plan — and it almost worked completely. But Odysseus shouted his real name as they escaped because he wanted the Cyclops to know who had beaten him. The Greeks called this hubris. Have you ever done something like this — undone a success by needing credit for it? What drove him to do it?

Page 14

🎉 Day 114 Complete!

Odysseus and the Cyclops

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Odysseus and the Cyclops
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed journal reflection

hubris

Definitions

Look up the definition of betrayal
Look up the definition of hubris
Look up the definition of hospitality
Look up the definition of cunning
Look up the definition of penance
Look up the definition of expedition
Look up the definition of oath

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Tone and Mood

Tone is the author's attitude toward the subject (serious, humorous, angry, hopeful, sarcastic).
Mood is the feeling the reader experiences (tense, peaceful, eerie, joyful, sad).

Example: "The dark corridor stretched endlessly before him" creates an eerie, tense mood.

Question 1:

What is the overall mood of this week\'s reading? Choose 2-3 mood words and support each with a specific quote or detail from the text.

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. boast

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 180).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Athena and Arachne

Athena and Arachne

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Arachne could weave.

Not just adequately. Not just well. She wove the way a musician plays when the music plays itself — with the particular effortless mastery that is so complete it looks like something other than skill. Her tapestries were famous throughout Lydia. People traveled from distant villages just to watch her work. They said no human had ever produced anything as beautiful.

They also said she must have learned from Athena, which they meant as the highest possible compliment.

Arachne did not receive it that way.

"I learned from no one," she said, every time. "My skill is my own. I would challenge Athena herself and win."

These words traveled, as boastful words do, until they reached Olympus.

An old woman appeared at Arachne's door one day — bent and white-haired, with sharp eyes that were doing more work than her posture suggested. She told Arachne gently that there was no shame in acknowledging a gift, that the gods were generous, that wisdom and talent alike had to come from somewhere, and that perhaps reconsideration was in order.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Athena and Arachne

Arachne told the old woman to mind her own business and repeat the challenge directly to Athena if she happened to see her.

The white hair and bent back fell away like a costume dropped to the floor.

"Very well," said Athena.

Two looms. Side by side. The same morning light. The same thread. One contest.

Athena wove the twelve Olympians in their glory — Zeus on his throne, Poseidon commanding the sea, all twelve gods in their full power and dignity. In the corners, small and deliberate, she wove scenes of mortals who had challenged gods and lost, a quiet warning stitched into the beauty.

Arachne wove something entirely different.

She wove the stories where the gods had behaved badly. Every time a god had used power carelessly, deceived someone weaker, acted without thought for who got hurt. Her tapestry was technically perfect — no one could find a flaw in it, not in the craft — and it made its point with every single thread.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Athena and Arachne

Athena looked at Arachne's tapestry for a long time.

The craft was flawless. This was the problem. If the tapestry had been bad, there would have been an easy answer. But it was perfect, and it was true, and it was a mirror held up to the gods' own stories.

Athena destroyed the tapestry. She struck Arachne with her shuttle. Whether it was the insult in the work or the perfection of the craft or both — the stories don't fully separate them.

Arachne, in despair, tried to hang herself.

Athena stopped her. She could not let Arachne die — but she could not let her remain entirely as she was. She transformed her into a spider: still weaving, always weaving, producing webs of extraordinary delicacy that have never been equaled.

The Greeks were honest about this story. Athena did not come out of it clearly in the right. Arachne's craft was real, her independence was real, and she'd been punished for both of them by someone who had the power to punish and chose to use it. The goddess of wisdom did not always behave wisely. The goddess of fair crafts was not always fair.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Athena and Arachne

The spider weaving in the corner has always been there. Arachne is still working.

The Greek word for spider is arachne.

Her name survived.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Arachne's tapestry was technically perfect. The only problem was what it depicted — the gods' bad behavior, shown truthfully and brilliantly. Was her punishment about her skill, her pride, or what she said? Can showing true things about powerful people be its own kind of dangerous act?

Page 14

🎉 Day 116 Complete!

Athena and Arachne

What You Accomplished Today:

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Athena and Arachne
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed journal reflection

📚 Week 36 Progress:

Vocabulary: 9 of 12 words learned

Assessment Day: Day 180 (Friday)

Day 117: 5th Grade - Lesson 117: Echo and Narcissus

Page 1

Lesson 117

5th Grade ELA

Greek Mythology

Echo and Narcissus

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 117!

Today you will:

  • Read from Echo and Narcissus
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

vanity

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (2 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 2

Words

1. boast
1. vanity

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 180).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 180).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Echo and Narcissus

Echo and Narcissus

A Tale from Ancient Greek Mythology

Echo loved to talk.

She had a gift for conversation — quick and warm and full of stories, the kind of person who could make a gathering of strangers feel like old friends within an hour. She was a nymph on the mountain, and she used her gift freely and well.

Perhaps too well.

Hera, queen of the gods, had been trying to catch her husband Zeus in the act of spending time with the mountain nymphs, which he did frequently. Every time Hera came looking, Echo would find her on the path and begin a conversation — long, interesting, genuinely engaging, running on just long enough for Zeus and whoever he was visiting to disappear before Hera arrived.

Eventually Hera understood what had been happening. She was not pleased.

"You love to speak," Hera said. "You may still speak. But only the last words of what others say. Never the first. Never your own."

Echo could only repeat.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Echo and Narcissus

She wandered the hills with her voice stolen from the inside out — all her thoughts and feelings locked in where no one could hear them, capable of speaking only when someone else gave her the words first. She could answer. She could not begin.

Then she saw Narcissus.

He was moving through the forest — a young man, hunting, calling to his companions — and he was extraordinarily beautiful. He was also, unfortunately, aware of it. He had been told about his beauty his whole life, and the attention had not made him kind. He found no one interesting enough to bother with.

Echo watched him through the trees and fell completely in love, the way you fall before you know someone — which is really falling in love with what you imagine they might be.

She followed him. When he called out, her voice came back from the hills: the last words he'd said, exactly.

He was intrigued. He called again. She answered. He asked who was there.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Echo and Narcissus

She stepped out of the trees and held her arms toward him.

He stepped back. He told her he wasn't interested, in terms unkind enough that he clearly meant them. He walked away.

Echo retreated into the caves and hollow places of the mountain. She faded — grew quieter, smaller, until there was nothing left of her but the voice. It's still there: in mountains and deep valleys, saying back to you the last thing you said.

Narcissus continued alone. One afternoon he found a spring, perfectly still, tucked in a shadowed hollow. He bent to drink.

He saw his own reflection.

He had never seen himself clearly before — not in a mirror like this, perfect and close. He reached toward the face in the water. It reached toward him. He spoke to it and the lips in the water moved. He had found, at last, someone who seemed as beautiful to him as he seemed to himself.

He could not leave. He stayed at the water's edge, reaching toward the face that reached toward him, speaking to the presence that could not speak back. Days passed. He did not eat. He did not sleep. He could not stop.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Echo and Narcissus

He died there, bent over his reflection, still reaching.

Where he died, a flower grew — white petals around a yellow center, the flower that still bears his name. Narcissus blooms in early spring, near water, bending toward its own reflection on the surface.

The Greeks told both stories together because they belong together: Echo, who had everything to say but no one to say it to. Narcissus, who had all the audience he needed but nothing real to love. Two different kinds of loneliness, meeting in a forest, and neither one able to give the other what they needed.

The voice in the hills is still Echo. The flower at the water's edge is still Narcissus.

They are still close to each other.

Still just missing.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Echo could only repeat what others said — she could never begin a conversation, only respond. What would that be like? What parts of yourself would you lose if you couldn't speak your own thoughts first? What do you think it cost Echo most — the words she couldn't say, or the connection she couldn't make?

Page 14

🎉 Day 117 Complete!

Echo and Narcissus

What You Accomplished Today:

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Echo and Narcissus
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced language skills
  • ✅ Completed writing practice

📚 Week 36 Progress:

Vocabulary: 12 of 12 words learned

Assessment Day: Tomorrow!

Day 119: 5th Grade - Lesson 119: Jupiter King of the Gods

Page 1

Lesson 119

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Jupiter King of the Gods

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 119!

Today you will:

  • Read from Jupiter King of the Gods
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

authority

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

invoke

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (7 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 7

Words

boast
vanity
obsession
fade
authority
invoke
divine

Definitions

A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
The right and power to make decisions and enforce them — legitimate power that others recognize and accept.
To call upon a god, spirit, or principle for help or witness — to formally name something as your support.
Of or belonging to the gods — having a sacred, superhuman quality.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Jupiter King of the Gods

Jupiter, King of the Gods

A Tale from Roman Mythology

The Romans did not simply borrow the Greek gods and put new names on them.

When they looked at Zeus and recognized Jupiter, when they saw in Poseidon the shape of Neptune, they were seeing family resemblances — related beings whose characters had developed differently, the way cousins share features but are not the same person. Jupiter was Zeus's Roman cousin, and the differences between them tell you something important about the difference between Greece and Rome.

Jupiter was the king of the gods — lord of the sky, master of the thunderbolt, the eagle his symbol and his vehicle. His great temple on the Capitoline Hill overlooked the Forum and the whole city of Rome. Every triumph ended there: every Roman general who had won a great victory processed through the city and climbed the hill to Jupiter's temple, because all Roman victories belonged ultimately to him.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Jupiter King of the Gods

He was not simply a storm god. He was the god of the state — of law and order and the proper structure of things. The Romans called him Jupiter Optimus Maximus: Jupiter, the Best and Greatest. His particular responsibility was Rome specifically, the divine guarantee behind Roman law and Roman order. When Roman generals made treaties, they swore by Jupiter. When senators spoke of Rome's destiny, they invoked Jupiter. He was the foundation everything else stood on.

His wife Juno was his equal in pride and ferocity if not in formal authority. Their marriage was what it is when two powerful people share a kingdom: complicated, contentious, and ultimately necessary. Juno was never simply Zeus's wife repackaged. She was a protector of marriage, of women, of the Roman state — fierce, long-memoried, and genuinely powerful.

His brothers were Neptune, who took the sea, and Pluto, who took the underworld. Jupiter took the sky and formal authority over all of it, though Neptune in particular tended to forget this when it suited him.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Jupiter King of the Gods

His children populated the Roman divine world. Minerva, goddess of practical wisdom and craft, sprang fully armored from his head. Mars, the god of war, was his son and deeply beloved in Rome — not as Ares had been in Greece, a necessary nuisance, but as a god of disciplined order, the force that protected civilization and extended it. Mercury was his messenger, quick and clever and available. Venus traced her divine lineage through him, and through Venus the Julian family claimed descent — Julius Caesar and Augustus presenting themselves as literally the children of gods.

The thunderstorm was Jupiter's most visible expression. When the sky darkened over Rome and the thunder rolled across the hills, Romans lifted their eyes. Not from fear, exactly — from recognition. The king of the gods was making himself present, reminding everyone that above the Senate and the legions and the laws and the emperors, there was something larger and older and more permanent that Rome answered to.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Jupiter King of the Gods

The Romans put Jupiter on the coins they used every day. They carved his eagle on their standards and carried him into every corner of the world they conquered. Wherever Rome went, Jupiter went with it — the divine backing of Roman order, the divine authority behind Roman law.

He was what the Romans wanted their empire to be: powerful, permanent, and ultimately just.

They built the greatest empire the western world had ever seen in his name. Whether he deserved the credit is a question the Romans left for philosophers.

They had an empire to run.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Jupiter was not just a storm god — he was the god of law and the proper structure of things. Why do you think the Romans needed their supreme god to be connected to law and order, not just power? What does it mean for a civilization to put its highest authority behind its legal system?

Page 16

🎉 Day 119 Complete!

Jupiter King of the Gods

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Jupiter King of the Gods
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Learned about Roman culture
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 120: Lesson 120: Week 24 Assessment

Page 1

Lesson 120

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Week 24 Assessment

fade
authority
invoke
divine

Definitions

Look up the definition of obsession
Of or belonging to the gods — having a sacred, superhuman quality.
Look up the definition of boast
Look up the definition of fade
To call upon a god, spirit, or principle for help or witness — to formally name something as your support.
Look up the definition of vanity
The right and power to make decisions and enforce them — legitimate power that others recognize and accept.

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Appositives

An appositive is a noun or noun phrase that renames or explains another noun right beside it. Set it off with commas.

Example: Arthur, the young king of England, pulled the sword from the stone.
Excalibur, a blade of extraordinary power, gleamed in the sunlight.

Question 1:

Identify the appositive in each sentence:

1. Merlin, the greatest wizard in the land, warned Arthur of danger. 2. The creature, a being of terrible loneliness, wept in the forest. 3. Sherwood Forest, Robin\'s beloved home, stretched for miles.

0 / 10 words
campaign

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
citizen-soldier

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (3 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 3

Words

discipline
campaign
citizen-soldier

Definitions

Controlled, orderly behavior maintained by training and habit — doing the right thing consistently, not just when you feel like it.
An organized military operation over a period of time — a planned series of military actions toward a goal.
A person who is both an ordinary member of society and a military defender when needed — the Roman ideal of civic duty.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Mars Father of Rome

Mars, Father of Rome

A Tale from Roman Mythology

The Greeks had Ares, and mostly found him alarming — chaotic, bloodthirsty, not the kind of divine presence you'd invite to a planning meeting. He was the god of the worst parts of war: the slaughter and the frenzy and the part where everything goes wrong at once. Even the other gods didn't like him much.

The Romans had Mars, and they loved him.

This difference tells you almost everything you need to know about the difference between Greece and Rome.

For the Romans, war was not chaos. War was order, imposed by force. War was how Rome brought its system — its roads and aqueducts and laws and architecture and the long Roman peace — to people who did not yet have these things. The Roman army was the most disciplined fighting force the ancient world had ever seen, and it functioned not by ferocity alone but by engineering and logistics and relentless, methodical organization. Mars was that army's divine patron, and the Romans were proud of him.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Mars Father of Rome

He was also, in their telling, the literal father of Rome. He fathered Romulus and Remus on a Vestal Virgin, and through Romulus he was the ancestor of every Roman. This was not incidental mythology. This was a foundational claim: Rome was descended from the god of war, and the capacity for organized, purposeful force was in Roman blood from the very beginning. The Romans built a theology around this, and then built one of history's largest empires around the theology.

The month of March is named for Mars. His month was when the military campaign season traditionally began — when the frozen ground thawed and the roads were passable again and armies could move. The Roman legions received their sacred weapons back from his temple each March, carried through the city in procession: the season of war has begun.

His temple in the Forum of Augustus was one of Rome's great buildings — tall white marble columns, a magnificent statue within, captured enemy standards brought here after every victory. Generals came to pray before departing on campaign. Young men received their adult togas in the Forum of Augustus, under Mars's eyes, because that transition from boy to citizen was also, in Rome, a transition toward the military responsibility of manhood.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Mars Father of Rome

But Mars was older than the city, and before he was the god of legions he was the god of fields. He was an agricultural deity in his oldest form — the protector of crops and flocks from wolves and bandits and the violent unpredictability of nature. The early Romans were farmers who could pick up weapons when needed and put them down when the need was past. Cincinnatus, called from his plow to serve as dictator in a crisis, led Rome to victory and returned to his plow the moment the job was done. This was the Roman ideal: the citizen-soldier, farmer and fighter, the sword a tool like the plow, taken up in service and laid down without ceremony.

Mars contained both — the man who worked the land and the man who defended it. The Romans saw no contradiction. The same steadiness that made a good farmer made a good soldier. The same discipline. The same willingness to do the necessary thing without fuss.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Mars Father of Rome

He was not the god of bloodlust. He was the god of the thing that Rome believed made civilization possible: the willingness to fight for what you had built, when fighting was what the situation required.

March is still his month. The martial arts, martial law, the martial virtues — all his name, still in the language, two thousand years after his last temple fell.

He is still here.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The Greeks found Ares unpleasant; the Romans loved Mars. The difference was how each culture thought about war — chaos vs. ordered force. What does the god a culture venerates tell you about what that culture values? What god would represent your own society's highest aspirations?

Page 15

🎉 Day 121 Complete!

Mars Father of Rome

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Mars Father of Rome
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 122: 5th Grade - Lesson 122: Venus Mother of Rome

Page 1

Lesson 122

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Venus Mother of Rome

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 122!

Today you will:

  • Read from Venus Mother of Rome
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

lineage

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
patron

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
civilizing

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (6 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 6

Words

discipline
campaign
citizen-soldier
lineage
patron
civilizing

Definitions

Controlled, orderly behavior maintained by training and habit — doing the right thing consistently, not just when you feel like it.
An organized military operation over a period of time — a planned series of military actions toward a goal.
A person who is both an ordinary member of society and a military defender when needed — the Roman ideal of civic duty.
A line of descent — the chain of ancestors connecting a person to their origins.
A god or powerful person who especially protects and supports a particular place, group, or cause.
Bringing culture, refinement, and the arts to people or places — the process of developing human society beyond bare survival.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Venus Mother of Rome

Venus, Mother of Rome

A Tale from Roman Mythology

Venus was the goddess of love.

The Romans meant this differently than we usually do. Love, in Venus's domain, was not only the soft, private feeling between two people. Love was the force that held the world together — the thing that made atoms cohere and plants grow toward light and creatures care for their offspring and civilizations build beautiful things when they could simply have built functional ones. Lucretius, the Roman poet, opened his great poem about the nature of the universe with an address to Venus: she was, he said, the pleasure of gods and men, the one through whom all living things were brought into being.

This was the goddess who was also Rome's mother.

Her son Aeneas was the ancestor of Rome. Through Aeneas's descendants, the Julian family claimed direct divine descent — Julius Caesar and Augustus presenting themselves as the children of Venus, not metaphorically but in the serious genealogical way the Romans took seriously. When Caesar stood in the Senate and when Augustus addressed the Roman people, they were, in the Roman understanding, literally speaking as the children of a goddess.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Venus Mother of Rome

Caesar built the Temple of Venus Genetrix — Venus the Ancestor, Venus the Origin — in the center of Rome, dedicating it in 46 BC. The statue inside showed her in full divine authority: not draped in soft romance but standing in the dignity of a divine mother, the origin of the Roman people displayed in marble for everyone who entered the Forum to see.

Augustus, building his own Forum beside Caesar's, placed Mars and Venus as the two presiding figures — the war god and the love goddess, the divine father of Romulus and the divine mother of Aeneas. The Romans chose these two as their ancestors. Not wisdom alone, not power alone — war and love, force and beauty, the capacity to destroy and the capacity to create, held together.

They believed this combination was civilization. Force without love was tyranny. Love without force was helplessness. Together, directed by reason and law, they made Rome.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Venus Mother of Rome

Venus in the stories was clever and protective and not above arranging things to suit her purposes. She used Cupid to make Dido fall genuinely in love with Aeneas, because her son needed safe harbor and she was not going to leave that to chance. She came to Aeneas in disguise on the Libyan shore and told him honestly that his road was going to be hard and that she would be watching.

The Romans did not find this troubling. Mothers do what they must for their children. Divine mothers have more options.

She was also the patron of beauty in every form — of the art and music and the beautiful public spaces and the magnificent buildings that Rome filled the world with, at enormous expense, when purely functional buildings would have served. The Romans, who were justly famous for their engineering, were equally famous for making their engineering beautiful, and they credited Venus with that impulse. Beauty was not weakness. Beauty was divine.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Venus Mother of Rome

April is her month — the month of opening, of blossoming, of things unfolding into what they were always going to be.

The Romans named a day of the week after her: Friday. Vendredi in French, Viernes in Spanish — Venus's day, still.

She is still everywhere. She always was.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The Romans chose war and love — Mars and Venus — as their divine ancestors. Not wisdom, not justice, not strength alone. What do you think they meant by that pairing? Is there something that force and beauty together create that neither alone can?

Page 15

🎉 Day 122 Complete!

Venus Mother of Rome

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Venus Mother of Rome
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced language skills
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 124: 5th Grade - Lesson 124: Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

5th Grade ELA
