Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 124!

Today you will:

  • Read from Minerva and the Arts of Civilization
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

competence

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
patron

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
discipline

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. competence

Definitions

Quiz on Friday (Day 190)!

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

A Tale from Roman Mythology

If you wanted to understand what the Romans valued most, you could do worse than looking at Minerva — what she governed, what she protected, and who prayed to her.

She was the goddess of wisdom, yes — the Roman version of Athena, from whom she descended. But Minerva's wisdom was specifically practical. She was the patron of craft: of weaving and pottery and metalwork, of the skilled hands that made things civilization required. She was the patron of teachers and their students. She was the patron of doctors who applied knowledge in service of healing. She was the patron of poets who applied skill in service of beauty. She was the patron of war, but of the planned and purposeful kind — the war that served a goal rather than the war that simply destroyed.

She was, in short, the divine patron of competence.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

This matters. The Romans could have made the patron of wisdom an ethereal figure concerned with pure thought and heavenly contemplation. Instead they made her the goddess of people who were very good at their jobs. The weaver at her loom, the surgeon at the operating table, the architect drawing plans, the general planning a campaign — all of them under the same divine protection, all of them doing the same essential thing: applying knowledge with skill, carefully, in service of something real.

Her festival Quinquatria ran five days in March. The first day was solemn and sacred. The next four were given over to gladiatorial games — not because Minerva was violent, but because the Romans understood that physical skill and discipline belonged to her domain alongside intellectual skill. The gladiators who performed during her festival were at the peak of their craft. Their precision and training were as much her province as a weaver's precision or a doctor's training.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

Schools held examinations during her festival. Artisans made offerings at her shrines. Soldiers had their weapons blessed. Doctors reviewed their instruments. The festival was a celebration of everything human beings could do well when they applied mind and hand together.

She was born, like Athena, from Jupiter's head — fully formed, fully armored, arriving as a complete adult with no childhood. The Romans found this origin meaningful: wisdom arrives complete, in a moment of clarity, like a thought that suddenly makes everything else make sense.

Her symbol was the owl, which sees in darkness and can turn its head to see what others miss. The Romans stamped owls on coins and carved them into architecture. That image — the owl as symbol of intelligence — is so old that we still feel it. You still put an owl on a diploma or a library or a school crest, two thousand years after the Romans stopped minting coins with Minerva's face.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

She had no husband, no romantic life, no children. This was deliberate, and the Romans noted it: Minerva was complete in herself, defined entirely by what she knew and what she could do. In a world where women were primarily understood through their relationships to men, Minerva stood apart as a divine being whose identity needed no one else.

She was what you prayed to when you were about to do something difficult that required both knowledge and nerve. When you weren't sure if you were good enough. When the work mattered and you needed to be better than you'd been before.

The owl sees in the dark. Minerva watches.

Do the work.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Minerva was the patron of both a surgeon's careful hands and a gladiator's trained body — intellectual skill and physical skill under the same divine protection. Do you think those two things are really the same kind of excellence? What do they have in common?

Page 14

🎉 Day 124 Complete!

Minerva and the Arts of Civilization

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Minerva and the Arts of Civilization
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 125: Lesson 125: Week 25 Assessment

Page 1

Lesson 125

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Week 25 Assessment

Page 2

🎮 Vocabulary Review

Match all 7 vocabulary words from this week!

Score: 0 / 7

Words

discipline
campaign
citizen-soldier
lineage
patron
civilizing
competence

Definitions

Bringing culture, refinement, and the arts to people or places — the process of developing human society beyond bare survival.
Controlled, orderly behavior maintained by training and habit — doing the right thing consistently, not just when you feel like it.
A line of descent — the chain of ancestors connecting a person to their origins.
A person who is both an ordinary member of society and a military defender when needed — the Roman ideal of civic duty.
A god or powerful person who especially protects and supports a particular place, group, or cause.
Look up the definition of competence
An organized military operation over a period of time — a planned series of military actions toward a goal.

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Complex Sentences

A complex sentence combines an independent clause (complete thought) with a dependent clause (incomplete thought) using words like because, although, while, when, if, since.

Example: Although Robin was outnumbered, he refused to surrender.

Question 1:

Combine these two sentences into one complex sentence using a connecting word (because, although, when, while, if, since):

The knight drew his sword. He saw danger approaching.

0 / 10 words
sufferance

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
trident

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. domain

Definitions

Quiz on Friday (Day 190)!

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Neptune and the Sea

Neptune and the Sea

A Tale from Roman Mythology

Neptune was not the god you prayed to when everything was going well.

He was the god you prayed to when the sky turned dark and the waves came up and your ship was very small and the sea was very large and there was nothing between you and the bottom of the ocean except the wood under your feet and whatever goodwill the god of the sea chose to show.

In this way, Neptune was one of the most honestly worshipped gods in the Roman world. You did not pray to him out of gratitude or civic duty. You prayed to him because you needed him, specifically and urgently, right now, and you knew it.

He was Jupiter's brother — one of the three sons of Saturn who had divided the world between them. Jupiter took the sky. Pluto took the underworld. Neptune took everything wet: the sea, the rivers, the lakes, the underground springs, and the earthquakes that shook the land when the water beneath it moved. His trident could split the earth open or raise storms or still the seas with equal authority. He drove a chariot across the ocean drawn by hippocampi — creatures with a horse's head and a fish's tail — and where he passed dolphins leaped around his wheels.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Neptune and the Sea

He was passionate and proud and had a remarkable gift for being second.

He competed with Minerva for the patronage of Athens. He drove his trident into a rock on the Acropolis and a saltwater spring poured out. Minerva planted an olive tree. The gods voted: the olive tree, which gave oil and food and wood and shade and lasted for centuries, won over the saltwater spring. Neptune sent floods to the region to express his feelings. The city was named for Minerva's Greek counterpart anyway.

He competed for Corinth. He lost. He competed for Aegina. He lost. He competed for several other cities and lost those too.

What he did not lose was the sea itself, which was more than enough. Rome was a Mediterranean power, and everything Rome ever traded, conquered, or supplied that crossed water crossed Neptune's territory. The grain ships from Egypt that fed Rome's millions. The war galleys that enforced Roman power from Britain to Persia. The merchant vessels loaded with wine and oil and amber and silk. All of them sailing on Neptune's sufferance.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Neptune and the Sea

He held his festival, Neptunalia, in late July — the hottest and driest time of year in Rome, when rivers ran low and springs dried up. The Romans built shelters from tree branches, feasted outdoors, and prayed for water. They gathered to celebrate the god of water in the season when they missed him most, which is the kind of honestly desperate worship that produces genuine feeling.

He also created the horse, which the Romans found slightly mysterious — what did the god of the sea have to do with horses? They decided that in the very beginning, when Neptune first struck the earth with his trident, what came out was not water but a horse. His power was force released from depth: whether that was a wave or a galloping horse was, at the fundamental level, the same thing.

When your ship is small and the sea is large, you pray to Neptune.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Neptune and the Sea

When the prayer works, you are grateful. When it doesn't, the sea is Neptune's too, and he answers to no one.

That was always the honest deal. The Romans understood it and prayed anyway.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Neptune was a god you prayed to when you genuinely needed him — not out of habit or civic duty but out of real fear. The Romans considered this honest worship. Is there something more real about prayer in a crisis than prayer when everything is fine? What does urgency do to belief?

Page 14

🎉 Day 126 Complete!

Neptune and the Sea

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Neptune and the Sea
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 127: 5th Grade - Lesson 127: Vulcan the Divine Craftsman

Page 1

Lesson 127

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Vulcan the Divine Craftsman

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 127!

Today you will:

  • Read from Vulcan the Divine Craftsman
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

transform

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
forge

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
resilience

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (6 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 6

Words

domain
sufferance
trident
transform
forge
resilience

Definitions

A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
To change something completely from one form to another — especially to improve it or make it more useful.
A workshop where metal is heated and shaped — or the act of making something through heat and force.
The ability to recover from hardship and keep working — to be strengthened rather than broken by difficulty.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Vulcan the Divine Craftsman

Vulcan, the Divine Craftsman

A Tale from Roman Mythology

Below the volcanic islands off the coast of Sicily, the Romans said, was Vulcan's forge — the great workshop of the god of fire, where the smoke rose through vents in the earth and the glow of the furnaces could sometimes be seen from ships passing above.

The word volcano comes from his name. He was here before the word, and the word has carried him forward.

Vulcan was thrown from Olympus as a child — whether by Juno who was displeased with him, or by Jupiter in a rage over something Vulcan had done in his mother's defense, the stories disagree. He fell for a full day and landed on the island of Lemnos, where he lay injured among people who found him and cared for him.

He never fully healed. He walked with a limp for the rest of his divine life.

He did not return to Olympus angry. He returned with gifts.

He had spent his years among the Lemnians learning his craft, and by the time he returned he was the greatest craftsman in all of creation — the only major god whose domain was not a natural force but a made thing. The sea is Neptune's, but Neptune didn't build it. Fire belongs to Vulcan, but fire specifically in service of making something. He was the god of transformation: taking raw material and turning it into something better, something purposeful, something that would last.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Vulcan the Divine Craftsman

He made Jupiter's thunderbolts. The most powerful weapons in creation, the instruments of the king of the gods, came from a craftsman's forge. The Romans found this satisfying rather than surprising — they understood that power without craft is just force, that the thing that makes force useful is the skill applied to it.

He built the palaces of Olympus. He made the golden mechanical servants that attended him in his workshop — helpers that could think and move, thousands of years before anyone had a word for what they were. He made the armor of Achilles, at the request of the hero's mother, armor so magnificent it became famous in a story already full of famous things. He made the chains that held Prometheus to the mountain.

His wife was Venus. The most beautiful goddess married to the lame craftsman — the Romans acknowledged this pairing was surprising and told it honestly. Venus was not always faithful. Vulcan knew. He made a net of such fine golden links it was invisible, and caught Mars and Venus together, and called the other gods to witness.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Vulcan the Divine Craftsman

The other gods came and looked and some of them laughed and some of them were uncomfortable and the general consensus was that Vulcan had won a technical and moral victory while remaining, somehow, still Vulcan — still the one at the forge while the beautiful ones had the adventures.

This too was Roman. They respected craft. They built roads that lasted two thousand years and aqueducts that still stand and bridges that are still used. They understood at a deep level that the things that endure are made things — things that required knowledge and patience and skill and the willingness to do the work.

Vulcan did the work. He always did. He was lame and overlooked and married to someone who didn't stay and he went back to his forge and made something extraordinary with the next piece of metal.

Vulcanization — the process of treating rubber with heat to make it stronger — bears his name still. Discovered thousands of years after his last worshiper died. Someone looked at the process and recognized it: this is Vulcan's work. Transformation through heat. Raw material into something better.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Vulcan the Divine Craftsman

He is still in the language. He is still in the forge. He is still making things.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Vulcan was lame and overlooked and married to someone who didn't stay faithful — and he was also the most essential craftsman in the universe, maker of the thunderbolts that Jupiter depended on. What does the story say about the relationship between being underestimated and being genuinely powerful?

Page 15

🎉 Day 127 Complete!

Vulcan the Divine Craftsman

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Vulcan the Divine Craftsman
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced language skills
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 129: 5th Grade - Lesson 129: Romulus and Remus

Lesson 129

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Romulus and Remus

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 129!

Today you will:

  • Read from Romulus and Remus
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

augury

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (2 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 2

Words

1. domain
1. augury

Definitions

Quiz on Friday (Day 190)!
Quiz on Friday (Day 195)!

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Romulus and Remus

Romulus and Remus

A Tale from Roman Mythology

Rome's symbol is a wolf.

Not an eagle, not a lion, not a sun — a wolf, nursing two human boys. The Romans put her on their coins and their standards and their monuments, and they were proud of her, and they wanted you to understand what it meant: Rome came from something wild and fierce and dangerous, and it never entirely forgot that.

Here is the story.

A king named Numitor ruled the city of Alba Longa, in the hills south of where Rome would one day stand. His brother Amulius wanted the throne. He took it. To make sure no heir of Numitor's could challenge him, he forced Numitor's daughter Rhea Silvia into the priesthood — made her a Vestal Virgin, sworn to a life without marriage or children.

The god Mars had other plans.

Rhea Silvia gave birth to twin boys. She said their father was Mars. Amulius had them placed in a basket and set in the Tiber River to drown.

The basket floated. It came to rest at the foot of a fig tree on a hillside — the Ficus Ruminalis, the tree the Romans would mark as sacred for centuries. A she-wolf found it there. She had recently lost her own cubs and was full of milk. She looked at the two babies and did what the situation required: she fed them and kept them warm. A woodpecker brought food. Both the wolf and the woodpecker were sacred to Mars. The god, the Romans said, was watching.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Romulus and Remus

A shepherd named Faustulus found them eventually and brought them home to his wife. They raised the boys as their own. Their names were Romulus and Remus.

The boys grew up strong and restless and good at being in charge, which is a quality that makes life interesting when you don't yet know who you are. They led bands of young men through the hills, cleared bandits from the shepherd roads, protected the flocks. Remus was captured once in a raid and brought before the old displaced king Numitor. Something about the young man's bearing made Numitor ask careful questions. Faustulus arrived with the truth.

The brothers united with their grandfather, overthrew Amulius, and restored Numitor to his throne.

Then they went to build their own city. They were not made for other men's kingdoms.

They chose the seven hills along the Tiber and disagreed immediately about which one. They settled it by augury — watching the flight of birds for a sign from the gods. Remus saw six vultures from the Aventine Hill. Romulus saw twelve from the Palatine. Twelve was more. Romulus began to mark out his city's boundary, digging the sacred trench, turning up the first soil, building the first wall.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Romulus and Remus

Remus mocked the wall. It was small, he said. He jumped over it.

The Romans told this moment several ways. In some versions it is Romulus who kills him, furious at the insult to the sacred boundary. In others it is one of Romulus's men who strikes first. In all of them, Remus dies, and Romulus is responsible.

He grieved. The Romans were careful to tell you this — they did not make him a monster or pretend he felt nothing. He buried his brother with full honors and named a gate of the new city after him.

Then he built the city.

He called it Rome, after himself. He built its walls and its institutions and its first laws. He invited anyone who needed a fresh start — fugitives, exiles, runaways, people with nothing and nowhere else to go — to come and be Roman. Rome would take anyone willing to work and fight and belong to it.

This was Rome's first policy, and in many ways its most important one.

The city that conquered the world began as a place for people nobody else wanted.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Romulus and Remus

The wolf nursed them. That was the beginning. Everything else was built on that — on the idea that something wild and fierce had looked at two abandoned children and decided they were worth saving.

The Romans never forgot where they came from. They put the wolf on everything to make sure no one else forgot either.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Romulus killed his brother over a boundary violation and then grieved and buried him with full honors. Do you think grief changes the meaning of what he did — or does grief not change what something was? Can you mourn someone and still have caused their death?

Page 15

🎉 Day 129 Complete!

Romulus and Remus

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Romulus and Remus
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Learned about Roman culture
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Roman Mythology

transform

resilience

sacred
heir

Definitions

Look up the definition of sacred
Look up the definition of sufferance
Look up the definition of domain
A workshop where metal is heated and shaped — or the act of making something through heat and force.
To change something completely from one form to another — especially to improve it or make it more useful.
The ability to recover from hardship and keep working — to be strengthened rather than broken by difficulty.
Look up the definition of trident
Look up the definition of augury
Look up the definition of heir

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Context Clues

When you find an unfamiliar word, look at the words and sentences around it for clues. Context clues include definitions, examples, synonyms, antonyms, or explanations nearby.

Example: The knight was valiant — brave and courageous in the face of danger.

Question 1:

Read this sentence and use context clues to define the underlined word. Explain which clues helped you:

The forest was so dense that sunlight could barely reach the ground, and travelers often lost their way among the thick, tangled branches.

0 / 10 words
destiny

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
refugee

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. piety

Definitions

Quiz on Friday (Day 195)!

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy

A Tale from Roman Mythology

The Greeks had their heroes at Troy — Achilles, Odysseus, Ajax. The Romans had their own.

His name was Aeneas, son of the goddess Venus and a mortal man named Anchises. He was not the greatest warrior at Troy — that was Hector, who is dead before this story begins. But Aeneas was something Hector wasn't: a survivor. And the Romans, who understood that survival is its own kind of heroism, made him their founding myth.

He woke to flames.

Hector's ghost had come to him in a dream and told him clearly: Troy is burning. The gods have left. Take the sacred objects of the city. Take your family. Go. Find a new home. Build something that lasts. Then Aeneas woke and smoke was already in the air and the screaming had already started.

He found his aged father Anchises, who could not walk well, and lifted him onto his shoulders. He took his young son Ascanius by the hand. He told his wife Creusa to follow close behind.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy

He carried his father through burning streets. He held his son's hand. He reached the gate.

He looked back.

Creusa was not there.

He gave Ascanius to one of the others and went back — back into the burning city, calling her name into smoke and fire and the sounds of a civilization ending. Her ghost came to him in the ruins. She was already gone, she said. This was as it had to be. She would not be coming with him. He must take their son and go and trust the gods who had a plan for him. She said it clearly and without bitterness and told him not to grieve too long.

He came back to the gate without her.

The Romans told this scene carefully because it mattered to them: Aeneas did not choose duty over love. He went back for her. He looked for her in the burning city. It was loss, not abandonment. That distinction was everything.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy

He sailed from the ruins of Troy with a small fleet — several hundred survivors, the sacred objects of the city, his father on his back and his son at his side. What followed were years of wandering that tested every kind of patience. The gods were not in agreement about his future. His mother Venus protected him. Juno, who had backed the Greeks and hated Troy, made every leg of the journey harder than it needed to be.

He stopped at Crete, thinking it was his destination. A dream told him otherwise. He stopped at Epirus. He lost his father in Sicily — Anchises, who had ridden on his son's back out of burning Troy, died there quietly, and Aeneas buried him with full honors.

He was shipwrecked on the coast of North Africa, where a queen named Dido was building a new city.

He would stay there longer than he should have, and leave more painfully than either of them expected.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy

But that is the next part of the story. This part ends on the beach at Carthage: a man who had carried his father out of a burning city and lost his wife in the smoke and wandered for years and buried his father in a foreign country, looking at a shore he hadn't meant to land on, with a son growing up beside him and a destiny still somewhere ahead.

Aeneas. Not the greatest warrior. The one who kept going.

That was what the Romans wanted their founding hero to be.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Aeneas went back into the burning city to find Creusa — he didn't choose duty over love, he lost her despite trying. Why do you think the Romans were careful to make that distinction? What difference does it make whether Aeneas abandoned her or failed to save her?

Page 14

🎉 Day 131 Complete!

Aeneas and the Fall of Troy

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Aeneas and the Fall of Troy
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 132: 5th Grade - Lesson 132: Junos Wrath and Aeneass Journey

Page 1

Lesson 132

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Junos Wrath and Aeneass Journey

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 132!

Today you will:

  • Read from Junos Wrath and Aeneass Journey
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

inevitable

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
opposition

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
duty

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (6 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 6

Words

piety
destiny
refugee
inevitable
opposition
duty

Definitions

A vocabulary word
A vocabulary word
A vocabulary word
Certain to happen — impossible to prevent no matter what you do.
Active resistance or force working against you — someone or something blocking your progress.
Something you are obligated to do — an obligation that comes before personal preference.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Junos Wrath and Aeneass Journey

Juno's Wrath and Aeneas's Journey

A Tale from Roman Mythology

Juno knew what was coming.

The Fates had shown her: a city founded by Trojans, rising in Italy, that would one day grind Carthage — her beloved Carthage, the city she had poured her divine energy into, the greatest city in the Mediterranean that was not Rome — to absolute dust. She had seen it in the prophetic threads. She could not undo it. No god could undo the Fates.

But she could delay it. She could make the road as long and painful as possible.

She went to Aeolus, keeper of the winds, who stored the great storms in a mountain cave. She offered him a beautiful nymph for his wife. He unleashed his worst storms on Aeneas's fleet.

The sea tore apart. Ships were scattered. Some sank. Aeneas lost men and vessels and was driven far off course, ending up exactly where Juno had planned — on the African shore near Carthage, where she hoped he would stay, would build a life with the Carthaginian queen Dido, would forget Italy and his destiny entirely.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Junos Wrath and Aeneass Journey

Neptune, who governed the seas, was displeased that Juno had used his domain without asking. He calmed the water and gathered what remained of the fleet. But the damage was done.

Aeneas stood on the Libyan shore and counted his ships and his men. Then he turned to face them — cold and sick with grief on the inside, steady on the outside, because they were watching and they were frightened and someone had to hold.

He told them they were Trojans. They had survived a ten-year war and a burning city. The gods had promised them a future. They had faced worse.

He did not entirely believe this. He told that part to his mother Venus, who came to him in disguise on the shore, furious at what Juno had done. Venus was honest with him: the road ahead was going to be hard, Juno was going to keep fighting it, and there was no way to make it easy. What she could promise was that Jupiter's word held — Italy was real, Rome was real, it was coming.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Junos Wrath and Aeneass Journey

She sent Cupid to arrange Dido's welcome. The Trojans entered Carthage under the full warmth of a queen who had known what it meant to lose everything and be building something from nothing. She understood Aeneas in a way few people could.

He stayed too long.

The time in Carthage was real — a real shelter for people who had had very little of it. Aeneas's men ate proper meals and repaired their ships and rested. He and Dido found in each other something genuine, and the Romans acknowledged this honestly.

Then Mercury arrived with Jupiter's command: Italy. Now. The mission does not wait for comfort.

Aeneas told Dido he had to leave. He did not do it well. He should have done it better, and the Romans knew it. She had given him everything, and he stood on her shore preparing to sail away and told her the gods required it.

She watched his fleet disappear. She built a pyre.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Junos Wrath and Aeneass Journey

Aeneas, sailing north, saw smoke rising from the Carthaginian shore. He understood what it meant. He did not turn back.

He carried that with him into Italy.

The Romans told both sides of this story without flinching, because this was what they believed about duty and fate and the human cost of great purposes: the future that Aeneas was sailing toward was real and worth reaching. The grief he was leaving behind was also real. Neither fact made the other smaller.

He sailed north. Behind him, Carthage mourned.

Ahead of him, Rome waited to be born.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Juno knew from the beginning she couldn't win — the Fates had already decided. She fought anyway for years, making Aeneas's journey as hard as possible. Do you think fighting an inevitable outcome is brave, futile, or both? Is there ever value in resistance even when you know you'll lose?

Page 15

🎉 Day 132 Complete!

Day 134: 5th Grade - Lesson 134: Cupid and Psyche

Page 1

Lesson 134

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Cupid and Psyche

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 134!

Today you will:

  • Read from Cupid and Psyche
  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

ordeal

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

immortal

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
perseverance

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (2 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 2

Words

1. piety
1. ordeal

Definitions

Quiz on Friday (Day 195)!
Quiz on Friday (Day 200)!

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Cupid and Psyche

Cupid and Psyche

A Tale from Roman Mythology

There was once a king with three daughters, and the youngest was so beautiful that people began neglecting the shrines of Venus and worshipping Psyche instead.

Venus noticed.

She sent her son Cupid — the god of love, winged and armed with arrows that caused whoever they struck to fall irresistibly in love — to find Psyche and make her fall in love with the most wretched creature alive. This was meant as a punishment for the girl's beauty drawing away worship that belonged to a goddess.

But when Cupid saw Psyche, he scratched himself with his own arrow — by accident, or by choice; the story is deliberately ambiguous — and fell in love with her instead.

Psyche's family received a terrifying oracle: she was destined to marry a creature that even the gods feared. She was to be dressed as a bride and left on a mountain and wait. The family wept and carried out the oracle. She stood on the mountaintop alone.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Cupid and Psyche

The west wind Zephyr came softly and carried her down to a valley where a palace stood — built by divine hands, attended by invisible servants, furnished with everything a person could want. A voice came to her in the dark each night: her husband, who told her she could have everything except one thing. She must never see his face.

Psyche was happy and also, eventually, lonely. She asked if her sisters could visit. Her husband warned her quietly that this was a bad idea. She insisted. The sisters came, saw the palace and the invisible servants and the extraordinary life, and were consumed by envy dressed as concern. Her husband must be a monster, they said — why else would he hide? They urged her urgently to take a lamp and a knife to bed that night.

She did.

The lamp showed her Cupid — not a monster but the most beautiful being she had ever seen, golden-winged, peacefully asleep. The lamp shook in her hands. A drop of hot oil fell on his shoulder.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Cupid and Psyche

He woke. He saw the lamp and the knife. He looked at her for a moment and said nothing.

He left.

The palace vanished. Psyche was alone in a field with her life dissolved around her.

She went to Venus directly and asked what she had to do. Venus, who was still angry, gave her four tasks, each impossible.

Sort an enormous pile of mixed seeds by morning. Psyche wept, and ants sorted the seeds for her. Gather golden wool from violent sheep. Psyche wept, and a reed by the river told her to wait until the sheep slept and gather the wool from the thorns. Fill a crystal vessel from an unreachable waterfall guarded by dragons. Psyche wept, and Jupiter's eagle took the vessel and filled it for her. Go to the underworld and bring back a box of Proserpina's beauty.

Psyche did not weep at this one. She went.

She did everything she was told to do in the underworld — the instructions had been specific — until the very last moment, on the way back out, when she opened the box.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Cupid and Psyche

Deadly sleep fell over her like a stone.

Cupid found her on the road. He woke her. He went to Jupiter and asked permission to make Psyche immortal. Jupiter agreed — and took the opportunity to settle the whole affair properly, making the marriage official and giving Venus grandchildren to be pleased about.

Psyche was made a goddess. She and Cupid were married on Olympus.

Her name means soul. His means love. The Romans, who thought carefully about what stories meant, were deliberate about this: the story of Cupid and Psyche is the story of love and the soul finding each other — and the point is that the finding requires going through everything. The tasks, the mistakes, the impossible journey to the underworld and back.

Love doesn't come to you. You go to it. And you have to be worth the trip.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Psyche opened the box from the underworld at the very last moment, after completing every other impossible task. The story doesn't fully explain why — curiosity? exhaustion? something else? Why do you think she did it? What is it about forbidden things that holds on even when you've come so far?

Page 14

🎉 Day 134 Complete!

Cupid and Psyche

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: Cupid and Psyche
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Day 135: Lesson 135: Week 27 Assessment

Lesson 135

5th Grade ELA

Roman Mythology

Week 27 Assessment

Page 2

🎮 Vocabulary Review

Match all 9 vocabulary words from this week!

Score: 0 / 9

Words

piety
destiny
refugee
inevitable
opposition
duty
ordeal
immortal
perseverance

Definitions

Look up the definition of perseverance
Look up the definition of immortal
Look up the definition of destiny
Look up the definition of ordeal
Look up the definition of piety
Look up the definition of refugee
Certain to happen — impossible to prevent no matter what you do.
Active resistance or force working against you — someone or something blocking your progress.
Something you are obligated to do — an obligation that comes before personal preference.

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Commas with Dependent Clauses

When a dependent clause comes before the main clause, use a comma. When it comes after, usually no comma is needed.

Example: ✅ Although he was afraid, he kept walking.
✅ He kept walking although he was afraid.

Question 1:

Add commas where needed in these sentences:

1. When the storm hit the travelers sought shelter. 2. The hero fought bravely because his friends were counting on him. 3. Before the sun rose the army was already marching.

0 / 10 words
precedent

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
republic

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. virtue

Definitions

Quiz on Friday (Day 200)!

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

The Story of Lucretia

The Story of Lucretia

A Tale from Roman History and Legend

Not all Roman myths were about gods. Some of the most important stories Rome told about itself were about human beings — about what Roman virtue meant, what it cost, and what it built.

The story of Lucretia is one of those. It ends with a woman's death, and it began the Roman Republic.

Lucretia lived during the time of the kings, when Rome was ruled by hereditary monarchs rather than elected officials. The king was Tarquinius Superbus — Tarquin the Proud — whose very name tells you what history thought of him.

A group of Roman noblemen on military campaign were sitting around a fire one evening, arguing about whose wife was most virtuous. They rode home unannounced to see for themselves.

Every other wife was found enjoying herself at a party.

Lucretia was found at home, spinning wool with her servants, working late by lamplight.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

The Story of Lucretia

By the Roman definition — which valued industry and household duty and seriousness of purpose — she was the most virtuous woman in Rome.

One of Tarquin's sons, Sextus Tarquinius, was in the group that night. He saw Lucretia and formed a terrible plan. Some nights later, he arrived at her house as a guest. She received him properly and gave him a room. In the dark, he came to her with a sword.

He told her that if she refused him, he would kill her and place a slave's body beside her and tell the world she had been found in disgrace. He had calculated precisely: a Roman noblewoman's honor was her family's honor, inseparable from it. Apparent disgrace would bring shame not only on her but on everyone connected to her.

She submitted rather than let that happen to the people she loved.

In the morning, she sent for her father and her husband. When they arrived, she told them exactly what had happened — named Sextus Tarquinius clearly, described what he had done, and named him responsible. Her father and her husband told her she had done nothing wrong. They begged her not to do what she was clearly planning.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

The Story of Lucretia

She had made her decision before they arrived. She said: no unchaste woman shall ever use Lucretia as her precedent.

She died by her own hand.

Her father and her husband took her body to the Roman Forum and displayed it to the people, naming Sextus Tarquinius and what he had done. A man named Junius Brutus — who had survived Tarquin's court by pretending to be a fool — stood beside the body and spoke. He named the whole family's corruption. He called for the expulsion of the kings.

Rome rose. Tarquinius Superbus and his family were driven out of the city. The monarchy ended.

The Roman Republic began.

Lucretia's story has been told and argued about for two and a half thousand years. The arguments are real and worth having: what she did was not required, what happened to her was not her fault, the system that made her feel she had no other choice was the system that killed her. These things are true.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

The Story of Lucretia

And also true: one woman's refusal to let her death mean nothing — her deliberate choice of witnesses, of timing, of last words — brought down a dynasty and changed the form of government that Rome and every civilization descended from Rome would use for the next five hundred years and beyond.

The Republic that produced the Senate, the consuls, the concept of elected government, the laws that became the foundation of Western legal tradition — it began with Lucretia, in the Forum, on the morning after.

She did not survive what happened to her. What she chose to do with it survived everything.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Lucretia called witnesses, named her attacker, told the full truth, and died making a statement. She turned what happened to her into a public act. Why do you think the Romans considered this the highest form of Roman virtue — using your death as testimony? What does it say about what they valued?

Page 14

🎉 Day 136 Complete!

The Story of Lucretia

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: The Story of Lucretia
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed writing

Page 2

  • Learn 3 new vocabulary words
  • Answer reading comprehension questions
  • Read an informational text
  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

plebeian

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (2 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 2

Words

1. virtue
1. plebeian

Definitions

Quiz on Friday (Day 200)!
Quiz on Friday (Day 200)!

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law

A Tale from Roman History

This is a different kind of story — not gods or heroes, but an idea. And the Romans would have told you, without hesitation, that this idea was more powerful than any god.

The idea was the law. Written down. Displayed in public. Applying to everyone equally, or at least equally enough that anyone could see what it said and point to it.

In the early years of the Roman Republic, after the kings had been expelled, Rome was governed by two groups: the patricians — the old noble families who had always held power — and the plebeians, who were everyone else. Farmers, craftsmen, traders, soldiers, the people who built and fought and fed the city.

The patricians held the important positions. They were the magistrates who applied the law. The priests who interpreted the gods. The senators who made decisions. And crucially, they were the ones who knew what the law was — because the law was not written down anywhere. It lived in the memory and tradition of patrician families. If you were a plebeian with a legal problem, you went to a patrician magistrate and hoped he was honest.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law

Many were not.

The plebeians went on strike. This is not a metaphor. They left the city — withdrew to a hill outside Rome and refused to return. Rome could not function without them. The patricians, who needed plebeian soldiers and farmers and craftsmen, were forced to negotiate.

The plebeians demanded many things over many years of struggle. The most fundamental demand was the simplest: write the law down.

Around 450 BC, Rome sent a delegation to Greece to study the laws of Solon and other Greek lawgivers. When they returned, a committee of ten men produced twelve bronze tablets and set them up in the Roman Forum, in the most public place in the city, for every Roman citizen to read.

The tablets were not perfect. Some of their rules are harsh by any standard. They did not give patricians and plebeians equal rights in every matter. But they were visible. They were public. Everyone knew what they said.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law

A magistrate could no longer claim the law meant whatever he found convenient. The law was there, on bronze, in the Forum. Go read it.

This seems obvious now because we have inherited it so completely that we cannot imagine a world without it. But in the ancient world, most power was held by people who could define the rules, because nobody else could check them. The Twelve Tables broke that monopoly. They took the law out of patrician memory and put it in the Forum where anyone could see.

The tablets were eventually destroyed — the Gauls sacked Rome in 390 BC and burned much of the city. But the Romans had memorized them. Roman children memorized the Twelve Tables as part of their education for centuries afterward, the way other cultures memorized sacred texts, because the Romans considered them that important.

From the Twelve Tables came all of Roman law. From Roman law came the legal tradition of every country that inherited the Roman world. The concept of written law, of public law, of law as something that exists independently of the people currently applying it — this is Rome's greatest and most lasting contribution to human civilization.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law

The Gauls burned the bronze. They could not burn what the Romans had already put in their children's heads.

The law survived. It always does, when enough people decide it matters.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The plebeians went on strike — they left the city and refused to return until they were heard. It worked. The patricians needed them more than the other way around. What does this tell us about where real power actually lives in a society — with the people who hold the official titles, or with the people who do the essential work?

Page 14

🎉 Day 137 Complete!

The Twelve Tables and Roman Law

What You Accomplished:

  • ✅ Learned 3 vocabulary words
  • ✅ Read: The Twelve Tables and Roman Law
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced language skills
  • ✅ Completed writing

  • Practice grammar and writing skills

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Vocabulary Words

void

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. void

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 145).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

A Tale from

In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

A Tale from Before the world existed, there were exactly two things. And they hated each other.

In the north: Niflheim. Ice. Darkness. Fog so cold it bit. Eleven rivers that crawled outward from a spring called Hvergelmir and froze solid before they got anywhere interesting. If you had stood at the edge of Niflheim — which you couldn't, because you didn't exist yet — you would have felt your eyelashes freeze off within seconds.

In the south: Muspelheim. Fire. Roaring, howling, everything-is-on-fire fire. A giant named Surt stood at its border holding a flaming sword, waiting for the end of the world with the patience of someone who has been waiting since before the world started and doesn't mind waiting until after it ends.

Between them: nothing. A yawning gap called Ginnungagap. The great empty nothing.

Then the cold crept south. The fire crept north. They met in the middle.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

The ice melted.

And from that melting, something enormous hauled itself upright, shook the frost from its beard, and blinked.

His name was Ymir. He was the first frost giant — bad-tempered, enormous, and not particularly bright. While he slept, other frost giants sweated out of him the way breath fogs in cold air, and those giants had more giants, and suddenly the empty void was full of enormous bickering creatures.

Also crawling out of the melting ice was a cow. An enormous, untroubled cow named Audhumbla. She had four udders and she fed Ymir with her milk, and she fed herself by licking the salty ice.

As Audhumbla licked, something started appearing in the block she was working on. Day one: hair. Day two: a head. Day three: a full person stepped out of the ice, blinking, looking around at the frost giants with an expression that said clearly: I am not one of these.

His name was Buri. He was the first of the gods.

Buri had a son named Bor. Bor had three sons: Odin, Vili, and Ve. The three brothers looked at Ymir snoring in the middle of everything, frost giants multiplying in every direction, and had a conversation that went approximately like this:

"We should build something better."

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

"Agreed."

"We'll need materials."

They looked at Ymir.

Ymir did not survive the meeting.

From his body, the brothers made the world. His flesh became the earth — all of it, every hill and plain and valley. His blood became the oceans and rivers. His bones became mountains. His teeth became boulders. His skull became the dome of the sky, which they propped up at the four corners with four dwarves named North, South, East, and West, who have been standing there ever since holding up the sky and presumably wishing they had said no.

His brains became clouds. His eyebrows became a great wall around the middle world to keep out whatever was left of the giants.

Then the brothers found two trees lying on the ground — an ash and an elm, just lying there, not doing much. Odin breathed life into them. Vili gave them understanding and the ability to move. Ve gave them senses, voices, color in their faces.

From the ash came a man named Ask. From the elm came a woman named Embla. They looked at each other. They looked at the world — the mountains made of bones, the sea made of blood, the sky made of a dead giant's skull — and somehow found it beautiful.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

The brothers built their own realm above: Asgard, the home of the gods, connected to the human world of Midgard by a rainbow bridge called Bifrost. They built great halls. They lit the sky with sparks from Muspelheim. They set the sun and moon running across the sky, chased by wolves who would catch them at the end of everything.

And holding all of it together — all nine worlds, from the fiery south to the frozen north, from the gods at the top to the dead at the bottom — was Yggdrasil, the World Tree. An ash so enormous that its branches sheltered the gods and its roots reached into three different wells. A squirrel named Ratatoskr spent every single day running up and down its trunk carrying insults between the eagle at the top and the serpent gnawing at the bottom.

Nobody asked him to do this. He just did it.

The world was built. The people were walking around in it. The gods were in their hall.

And somewhere at the border of Muspelheim, Surt was still standing, still holding his flaming sword, still waiting.

He was very patient.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Before the world existed, two very different places faced each other across an empty void. What were they, and what made each one so extreme? What happened when they finally met in the middle?

Page 15

🎉 Day 139 Complete!

In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

  • ✅ Read In the Beginning: The Norse Creation

  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed journal reflection

precedent

primordial
realm

Look up the definition of realm
Look up the definition of plebeian
Look up the definition of void
Look up the definition of codify
Look up the definition of transparent
Look up the definition of primordial
Look up the definition of republic
Look up the definition of virtue
Look up the definition of precedent

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Greek and Latin Roots

Many English words come from Greek and Latin roots. Knowing these roots helps you figure out unfamiliar words.

Example: trans- = across (transport, translate) • morph = form (metamorphosis) • bene- = good (benefit, benevolent)

Question 1:

Break these words into their root parts and explain what each part means:

1. incredible (in- + cred + -ible) 2. transform (trans- + form) 3. submarine (sub- + marine)

0 / 10 words
prophecy

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
runes

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (1 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 1

Words

1. sacrifice

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 145).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Odin and the Price of Wisdom

Odin and the Price of Wisdom

A Tale from

Odin and the Price of Wisdom

A Tale from Odin wanted to know everything.

This was not a casual interest. This was a deep, restless, never-satisfied hunger that had been with him since before the world was built. He had two ravens — Huginn and Muninn, Thought and Memory — who flew across all nine worlds every single day and came back to his shoulders at night to whisper everything they'd seen. He knew what the frost giants were arguing about. He knew which humans were planning wars. He knew what the dwarves were crafting in their deep halls.

It wasn't enough.

There was a well at the root of Yggdrasil, the World Tree — a spring so deep and so old that its water had soaked in the wisdom of everything that had ever happened, every secret, every truth, every answer to every question anyone had ever asked or would ever ask. It was called Mimir's Well, and the ancient being who guarded it sat beside it every morning and drank a cup of its water, which is why Mimir was the wisest person in any of the nine worlds.

Odin had to have it.

He walked the long road from Asgard down through the roots of Yggdrasil to the cold dark where Mimir's Well sat, black and still, reflecting nothing. Mimir looked up from his cup. He was very old. He was not impressed by visitors.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Odin and the Price of Wisdom

"I want to drink from the well," said Odin.

"I know," said Mimir. "The price is one of your eyes."

Odin didn't flinch. He'd known there would be a price — real wisdom always had one. He thought about what it meant. He thought about going through the rest of existence seeing the world through one eye instead of two.

Then he reached up and paid.

He handed the eye to Mimir, who placed it in the well, where it sank slowly into the dark water. Down there in the cold depths, it would see everything — every corner of every world, forever. Odin would always wonder what it was seeing that he wasn't.

He drank.

It was like being struck by lightning from the inside.

He staggered back from the well with knowledge so vast it took him a long time to find himself inside it. He had seen the future. He had seen Ragnarok — the end of everything — playing out in terrible detail. He knew how he would die. He knew who would cause it. He knew he could not stop it.

And he knew it was worth it anyway.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Odin and the Price of Wisdom

But Odin wasn't done.

There were the runes. The runes were more than letters — they were the language the universe used to run itself, carved into the roots of Yggdrasil since before time had a name. They contained power that went beyond swords and strength: the power to heal, to harm, to understand the hidden meaning of things. Odin had seen them in flashes in the well's wisdom, but seeing them wasn't the same as knowing them.

To know them, he had to find them himself. And the only way to find them was to go to the place where they lived: the deepest dark of the World Tree's roots.

He climbed Yggdrasil. He tied himself to the trunk with a rope. He drove a spear through himself — a sacrifice of himself to himself, an offering to the magic that was older than the gods. And he hung there, looking down into the dark, with no food and no water and nothing but the cold and the pain and the long wait.

One day. Two days. Three.

The other gods passed below. None of them helped. This was the rule — he had to find the runes alone.

Day four. Day five.

Day six. His arms were numb. The cold had worked its way inside him.

Day seven. Day eight.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Odin and the Price of Wisdom

On the ninth night, in the extremity of everything he had given up, he saw them.

They came up from the roots like light rising through water — twenty-four shapes, each one burning with its own meaning. He grabbed them. He held on. He fell back from the tree with the knowledge of all of them blazing in his mind.

He shared them with gods and humans both. The runes — the writing, the magic, the deep understanding of the world — were a gift. A gift that had cost him an eye and nine days of hanging in the cold and dark.

He went back to Asgard with one eye and a mind full of secrets and his ravens landing on his shoulders like nothing unusual had happened.

"Anything interesting while I was gone?" he asked.

Huginn and Muninn told him everything.

It still wasn't quite enough.

There was always more to know.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Odin gave up his eye to drink from Mimir's Well — and then, later, he hung on the World Tree for nine days to find the runes. Why do you think wisdom required such extreme sacrifices? Do you think it was worth it? Why or why not?

Page 15

🎉 Day 141 Complete!

Odin and the Price of Wisdom

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Practiced with word scramble
  • ✅ Read Odin and the Price of Wisdom
  • ✅ Explored informational text
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed journal reflection

Assessment Day: Day 145 (Friday)

Day 142: 5th Grade - Lesson 142: Thor and the Theft of Mjölnir

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Thor and the Theft of Mjölnir

Thor and the Theft of Mjolnir

A Tale from

Thor and the Theft of Mjolnir

A Tale from Thor woke up in a terrible mood.

This was not unusual. Thor often woke up in a terrible mood. He was a large god with a large appetite and a large temper, and mornings were not his best time. He rolled out of his enormous bed, shook the rafters of Bilskirnir with a yawn that sounded like distant thunder, and reached for Mjolnir.

Mjolnir was not there.

Thor looked at the empty space where his hammer lived. He looked at it for a long moment, the way you look at a place where something should be, hoping that if you look long enough it will simply appear.

It did not appear.

"LOKI."

Loki appeared in the doorway, looking perfectly cheerful, which was suspicious on its own. "Good morning," he said.

"My hammer is gone."

Loki's eyebrows went up. He looked at the empty space. He looked at Thor. He had the expression of someone who was absolutely delighted by this development but had enough self-preservation instinct not to show it. "Gone," he repeated.

"Gone."

"Well," said Loki. "That's a problem."

"Find it."

Loki borrowed Freyja's falcon cloak — a feathered cape that transformed its wearer into a bird — and launched himself from the walls of Asgard. He flew for a long time, over the rainbow bridge, past the borders of the gods' realm, out across the vast grey stretches of Jotunheim, the land of the giants.

He found Thrym sitting on a hillside, cheerfully braiding the manes of his horses.

Thrym was enormous, even by giant standards — the kind of large that makes everything around it look like furniture for children. He had a beard like a storm cloud and eyes the color of dirty ice, and he was in a spectacularly good mood, which told Loki everything he needed to know.

"Thrym," said Loki, landing on a nearby rock and becoming himself again. "Nice morning."

"Wonderful morning," said Thrym. "Best morning I've had in years, actually."

"You wouldn't happen to know anything about a hammer."

Thrym smiled the smile of a giant who has hidden a hammer eight miles underground and feels very smug about it. "I've hidden Mjolnir eight miles underground," he said. "Nobody gets it back." He paused. "Unless."

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Thor and the Theft of Mjölnir

Loki waited.

"I want Freyja as my wife."

Loki flew back to Asgard.

He found Freyja in her hall and explained the situation. He barely got through the first sentence before her eyes went to a temperature that made the air around her crackle.

"Absolutely not," said Freyja.

"He just wants—"

"I heard what he wants." She stood up, and the floor of her hall shook slightly. Her famous necklace Brisingamen snapped from the force of her fury and clattered to the floor. "I am not going to Jotunheim. I am not marrying a giant. I would rather Mjolnir stay in the ground forever."

She sat back down and picked up her necklace.

"So that's a no," said Loki.

The gods held a council. Everyone agreed that losing Mjolnir permanently was not an option — it was the primary thing keeping the giants from simply walking into Asgard and breaking everything. They argued for a while about possible solutions, most of which were impractical, some of which were insulting, and one of which was suggested by Heimdall.

Heimdall guarded the rainbow bridge. He spent most of his time watching the horizon in all directions and thinking carefully, because there was nothing else to do, and centuries of careful thinking had made him very good at it.

"Thor should go as the bride," Heimdall said.

Everyone looked at Heimdall. Then everyone looked at Thor.

Thor had been sitting quietly during the council, which was itself unusual, his red beard bristling, his enormous fists on his knees. He had the expression of a man who was already beginning to understand where this conversation was going and did not like it even slightly.

"No," said Thor.

"The giants will—"

"No."

"Without Mjolnir, Asgard—"

"No," said Thor. "I am not dressing as a bride. I am not doing it. Find another way."

There was a long silence.

"There isn't another way," said Odin.

Thor looked at his father. He looked around at the council of gods, all of them watching him, the safety of Asgard depending on whether he was willing to put on a dress. He thought about Mjolnir in the dark underground, out of reach. He thought about giants walking through the gates.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Thor and the Theft of Mjölnir

He looked at Loki.

"You're coming with me," he said.

"Obviously," said Loki, who had been looking forward to this for some time.

The bridal dress was Freyja's idea, which was the least she could do given the circumstances. It was white and long and had complicated embroidery around the collar. Thor stood in the middle of the room in it, arms out, while the goddesses adjusted the veil and tried very hard not to laugh.

The veil helped. It helped considerably, covering the red beard and the expression.

"Keys at your belt," said Frigg, pinning a cluster of household keys to Thor's waist — the sign of a respectable Norse bride. "And hunch a little."

"I am not hunching."

"You are eight feet tall."

Thor hunched.

Loki, meanwhile, was dressed as a handmaiden in a practical blue dress, his dark hair braided, his expression one of pure angelic innocence that fooled no one who knew him and would hopefully fool the giants.

They climbed into a chariot. Thor's goats pulled them across the sky toward Jotunheim, which was significantly bumpier than Bifrost because Thor was driving and was not in a cooperative mood.

Thrym had prepared a feast. He was genuinely excited — tables groaning with food, great fires burning, his whole household assembled to witness his wedding. He stood at the door of his great hall as the chariot arrived and beamed.

"My bride!" he said. Thor said nothing. This was wise.

The feast began. The food kept coming and Thor kept eating. An entire ox disappeared. Eight full salmon. Every little cake and sweet set out for the women's table. Three barrels of mead, emptied one after another.

Thrym leaned toward Loki. "Your mistress," he said carefully, "has quite an appetite."

"She hasn't eaten in eight days," Loki said smoothly, without looking up from his own plate. "So eager was she for this wedding. She's barely slept either. Very eager. Couldn't think about anything else."

Thrym seemed reassured. He leaned over to kiss his bride.

He lifted the veil. He looked directly into Thor's eyes from a distance of about six inches.

He sat back very quickly.

"Her eyes," he said to Loki, his voice not quite steady. "They're like — they're burning. Like fire."

"She hasn't slept in eight days," Loki said. "Very eager."

Thrym looked at the veiled figure again. The figure sat very still and said nothing, which was working well.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Thor and the Theft of Mjölnir

"Right," said Thrym. "Yes. Bring in the hammer! Let us bless the bride."

It was the Norse tradition: a hammer placed in the bride's lap to bless the wedding and call on the gods for a fruitful marriage. Thrym had not thought this particular tradition through.

Four giants carried Mjolnir in from wherever it had been stored. It was heavy enough that all four of them were straining. They set it in the bride's lap.

There was a pause.

Then the hand that closed around the handle was not soft or small or remotely bridal.

The veil came off.

What happened next was not the wedding Thrym had planned. It was efficient, thorough, and left no confusion about whether Mjolnir had been recovered.

Thor and Loki flew home. Thor drove the chariot faster than he had driven it there, Mjolnir across his knees, his wedding dress in somewhat worse condition than when he had put it on.

"You were very convincing," said Loki.

Thor said nothing.

"The way you ate those salmon. Very bridal."

Thor continued to say nothing, with great intensity.

"I think Thrym genuinely believed—"

"Loki."

"Yes?"

"Never speak of this again."

Loki looked straight ahead at the horizon. His expression was the expression of a person who was absolutely going to speak of this again, many times, for the rest of eternity. "Of course," he said.

They crossed back over Bifrost. Heimdall, at his post, watched them arrive. He looked at Thor in the tattered wedding dress, hammer in hand, and said nothing at all, which was the most diplomatic thing he had ever done.

Mjolnir was home. Asgard was safe. Thor went directly to his hall and changed.

Somewhere in Jotunheim, the story was already spreading. The giants were laughing about it too, eventually. Not right away. But eventually.

Thor was not laughing. Thor would never admit to having found any of it funny.

Loki was laughing enough for both of them.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Thor absolutely did not want to dress as a bride — but he did it anyway. What finally convinced him? What does that decision tell you about what kind of god Thor really is underneath all the thunder and strength?

Page 16

🎉 Day 142 Complete!

Thor and the Theft of Mjölnir

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings

Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings

A Tale from

Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings

A Tale from Freyja was not someone you said no to.

She was the most powerful goddess in Asgard. She rode to battle on a chariot pulled by two enormous cats. She wore a cloak of falcon feathers that let her fly between worlds like a thought crossing the sky. Half the warriors who died in battle came to her hall, Sessrumnir — she chose first, before Odin even got a look. She was the goddess of love and war both, which tells you everything about what the Norse thought love and war had in common.

She was also, underneath all of that power, carrying a grief that never left. Her husband Od had disappeared on distant journeys long ago, and she had been searching for him ever since, weeping tears of red gold that fell wherever she walked. She was the most powerful person in any room she entered, and she was lonely, and she didn't let either one stop her from being exactly who she was.

Which is why, when she found the necklace, she had to have it.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings

She had been wandering through the deep places of the earth — the tunnels and caves where the dwarves made their extraordinary things — when she saw light coming from a cave she hadn't noticed before. She looked in.

Four dwarves. A forge blazing. And on a stone in the center of the cave, a necklace that seemed to be made of trapped sunlight — gold so perfectly worked, so brilliantly designed, so alive with its own light that Freyja felt it before she saw it.

The dwarves watched her watching it. Their names were the Brisings, and they had been working on that necklace for years.

"I want it," said Freyja. "Name your price."

The dwarves named it. It was deliberately insulting — a price designed to humiliate, to test whether her desire for the necklace was stronger than her pride.

Freyja looked at them. She looked at the necklace. She thought for a long moment.

"Fine," she said.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings

Four days later, Brisingamen was clasped around her neck. It caught the light of the forge fire and seemed to burn from within. She wore it out of the cave and back up into the world, and every creature she passed stopped and stared, and the necklace was warm against her throat, and it was worth it.

Back in Asgard, Loki found out about the transaction. Loki found out about everything eventually.

He went to Odin. Odin sent Loki to steal the necklace — not out of cruelty, but because he needed something from Freyja and Brisingamen was the only leverage in the world.

Loki turned into a fly. He found the single tiny crack in Freyja's locked hall and buzzed through it. Inside, Freyja was asleep with Brisingamen clasped around her neck, the clasp tucked underneath her, pressed to the floor.

Loki turned into a flea. He bit her until she shifted in her sleep, rolling onto her back. Then he became himself again, unclipped the necklace, and left before she woke.

Freyja's shout when she found it gone probably woke half of Asgard.

She went straight to Odin. She stood in front of him in all her fury — the goddess of war and love and the red-gold tears and the falcon cloak — and she asked him, in a voice that did not shake, what he wanted.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings

Odin told her the price: cause a battle between two kings. Keep it going. The dead rise every morning, the war never ends, until a hero breaks the cycle.

She did it. She got Brisingamen back. She clasped it around her neck and walked out of Odin's hall without looking back.

She was still searching for Od. She was still carrying the grief. She was still the most powerful person in any room.

She just also had the necklace now.

Some things are worth the price.

Some things you decide are worth the price, and then you pay it without looking down, and you don't let anyone see how much it cost.

Freyja knew exactly how to do both of those things.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The dwarves set a price for Brisingamen that was designed to be insulting — to test whether Freyja's pride was stronger than her desire. Freyja thought about it and then said yes. Do you think she made the right choice? What does her decision reveal about her character?

Page 15

🎉 Day 144 Complete!

Freyja and the Necklace of the Brisings

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Figurative Language: Similes & Metaphors

A simile compares two things using like or as. A metaphor makes a direct comparison without like/as.

Example: Simile: His voice was like thunder.
Metaphor: The forest was a cathedral of green.

Question 1:

Identify whether each example is a simile or metaphor and explain what is being compared:

1. The warrior\'s heart was a furnace of rage. 2. She moved through the crowd like a shadow. 3. His words cut like a knife.

0 / 10 words
