Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge

Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge

A Tale from

Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge

A Tale from At the edge of Asgard, where the realm of the gods ended and the rainbow bridge Bifrost began, there was a hall called Himinbjorg — Heaven's Cliff. It had a great view in every direction. It was drafty. The neighbors were either the gods, which was occasionally dramatic, or the giants trying to sneak across the bridge, which was regularly dramatic.

This was Heimdall's home, and he wouldn't have traded it for anything.

His job was the most important and the least celebrated in all of Asgard: watch. Watch the bridge. Watch the sky. Watch the horizon in every direction, at all times, in full darkness and thick fog and driving rain, without sleeping, without missing anything.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge

He was built for it. He could see a hundred miles in any direction. He could hear the grass growing in the fields of Midgard and the wool growing on the backs of sheep. He needed less sleep than a bird. He ate almost nothing — a little something in the morning, and then just watching, all day, all night.

In his hall he kept Gjallarhorn, the Resounding Horn. It was the loudest horn ever made. When he blew it, the sound would carry through all nine worlds at once — every creature in every realm would hear it and know that something had gone wrong at the gate.

He had never blown it.

He practiced, sometimes, just to keep his lungs ready. The gods could hear him practicing. Occasionally one of them would lean out a window and shout something, and Heimdall would shout back that he was practicing, and go back to watching.

He watched the Bifrost — the great rainbow bridge that arced between Asgard and Midgard, all seven colors blazing, treacherous to walk on if you weren't invited. He watched the giants in Jotunheim who sometimes drifted toward the bridge with the expression of things considering whether today was the day.

It was never the day. Heimdall made sure of that.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge

The Norse told a story about Heimdall once visiting the human world of Midgard in disguise — calling himself Rig, wandering from farmhouse to farmhouse, staying three nights in each home. In time, each household had a remarkable child. From the poorest family, a child who grew into a hard and capable worker. From the middle family, a child who became a skilled and steady farmer. From the finest household, a child who grew into a leader — quick-minded, strong, the kind of person other people followed.

Rig was the father of all the different kinds of people. He had walked through the human world and seeded it with possibility.

Then he went back to his post at the bridge.

Nobody gave him a feast for it. Nobody composed a great poem about the father of humanity standing at his drafty watchtower eating almost nothing and listening to the grass grow. The gods were in their great halls having adventures.

Heimdall watched the horizon.

He had one job. He did it every day, without complaint, without asking for recognition, because he understood something that was easy to forget when you were the one going on adventures: adventures are only possible because someone is watching the door.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge

Every time Thor charged off to fight giants, every time Odin rode out on his mysterious errands, every time Freyja flew between the worlds in her falcon cloak — Heimdall was at the bridge, making sure Asgard was still there when they came back.

He is still there, in the stories. Watching.

The horn is still in his hand.

It hasn't been blown yet.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Heimdall's entire job is to watch — every day, all day, all night, without sleep, without missing anything. He never goes on adventures. He just guards the bridge. Why do you think the Norse made him a god rather than just a very reliable guard? What does giving him divine status say about how they valued this kind of work?

Page 15

🎉 Day 146 Complete!

Heimdall and the Rainbow Bridge

Definitions

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 150).
Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 150).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Loki the Trickster

Loki the Trickster

A Tale from

Loki the Trickster

A Tale from If something in Asgard had gone wrong, Loki was probably involved. If something in Asgard had been saved at the last second in a very strange way, Loki was probably also involved. These two facts were deeply connected.

He wasn't a god, exactly — not by birth. He was the son of giants, brought into Asgard as a young man by Odin himself, bound to him by a blood oath that made them brothers. He was smarter than almost anyone in any of the nine worlds and he was bored enough to do something about it at all times.

He was a shape-shifter. He could be a hawk, a salmon, a fly, a gust of wind, an old woman with excellent gossip. He could become anyone and anything, and he usually did it at the worst possible moment for everyone except himself.

The story of how he caused a disaster and then fixed it by becoming a horse is a good example.

A builder arrived in Asgard one day with an offer: he would construct a great wall around all of Asgard — a wall that would keep every giant and every enemy out forever — in a single winter. His price was the sun, the moon, and the goddess Freyja.

The gods had a council. Obviously they couldn't pay that price. But what if he failed? If the wall wasn't done by the first day of summer, he'd get nothing. And surely no one could build an entire wall around Asgard in one winter.

Loki said it was a great deal.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Loki the Trickster

The gods agreed to the terms.

The builder started work. He had a horse helping him — a massive stallion named Svadilfari who could haul three times what any normal horse could carry, and who worked through the night without stopping. The wall grew. Day after day, stone by stone, the wall grew faster than anyone had expected.

Three days before summer. The wall was nearly done.

The gods turned to look at Loki.

Loki had the expression of someone who was already thinking.

"Fix it," said Odin.

"I will absolutely fix it," said Loki.

He went out into the woods near the building site and transformed himself into a mare — a beautiful, fast mare who appeared at the edge of the tree line just as Svadilfari was hauling the heaviest load of the night. The stallion looked over. He stopped. He looked again.

He dropped the stone and ran into the woods.

The builder ran after his horse, shouting. The builder found his horse. His horse did not want to be found. This went on all night. No stones were moved. No wall was built.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Loki the Trickster

Three days later, summer arrived. The wall was unfinished. The builder didn't get paid.

The builder was also, it turned out, a mountain giant in disguise, which was against the rules of the deal, so Thor dealt with him directly and the problem was resolved.

Loki came back from the forest several months later, leading a grey foal with eight legs.

Eight legs. The foal had eight legs.

"That's Svadilfari's," said Odin slowly.

"Yes," said Loki, in a tone that did not invite further questions.

Odin looked at the eight-legged foal for a long moment. It was clearly extraordinary — already bigger than it should be, moving with an eerie smoothness across the ground, as if gravity was a suggestion rather than a rule.

"I'll take him," said Odin.

The foal grew into Sleipnir — the greatest horse in all nine worlds, who could gallop between the realms, cross seas, run through the sky, and travel paths that no other creature could follow. He became Odin's horse and Odin's most valuable companion.

So: Loki made a bad deal, caused a crisis, fixed it by turning into a horse, and accidentally produced the best horse in existence.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Loki the Trickster

This was Loki on a pretty average week.

The trouble was that Loki's cleverness had no off switch. The same brain that solved impossible problems also noticed things that were better left unnoticed and said things that were better left unsaid. He cut off the golden hair of Thor's wife Sif one night — just because he could — and then spent the next month frantically bargaining with dwarves to replace it, gambling with his own head as the stakes.

He won. Barely. The dwarves sewed his lips shut instead of taking his head, which they argued was technically winning, and Loki argued was technically wrong, and neither side was entirely satisfied.

His lips came unsewn. His mouth was never quite shut after that.

He was Odin's blood brother and the gods' best problem-solver and the cause of most of the problems he solved. He was the one you called when everything was impossible and the one you watched very carefully when things were fine.

He was always both.

And somewhere in the back of every Asgardian's mind was the quiet knowledge that those two things — the helper and the danger — were the same person.

They always had been.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Loki caused most of the problems he ended up solving. Think about the wall-builder story: Loki agreed to the deal, which created the crisis, and then fixed it in the strangest possible way. Do you think the gods were right to keep trusting him after things like this? Why or why not?

Page 15

🎉 Day 147 Complete!

Loki the Trickster

Day 149: 5th Grade - Lesson 149: Týr and the Binding of Fenrir

Definitions

A vocabulary word from the story

A prediction of what will happen — the Norse took these very seriously.
To give something up — often as the cost of doing what's right.
Doing what's right even when it costs you something — following your principles all the way through.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Day 1 - Matching

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Týr and the Binding of Fenrir

Tyr and the Binding of Fenrir

A Tale from

Tyr and the Binding of Fenrir

A Tale from The wolf had a name: Fenrir. He was the son of Loki and a giantess named Angrboda, and the gods had brought him to Asgard when he was a pup because a wolf pup of divine parentage seemed manageable.

He was not manageable for long.

He grew the way only magical things grow — not slowly and predictably but in lurches, so that one week he was large and the next week he was enormous and the week after that the gods looked at each other and had an uncomfortable conversation about what they were going to do.

The prophecy was specific: Fenrir would swallow Odin at Ragnarok. They couldn't kill him — not before his time, not like this. But they could try to contain him.

Only one god would feed Fenrir. Only one god went to the wolf's pen every day, walked right up to those massive jaws, and set down enough meat to fill a horse. His name was Tyr, and he was the god of justice and law, and he was the bravest of all the Aesir in the particular way that matters most: calmly, quietly, without making a show of it.

Fenrir let Tyr feed him. Something about the god who came every day, on time, who was honest with him and fair, had earned a kind of trust.

This is important for what came next.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Týr and the Binding of Fenrir

The gods tried chains first. They made enormous iron chains, presented them to Fenrir as a test of strength. The wolf looked at the chains. He let them put them on. He flexed his muscles, slowly, and the chains snapped.

Everyone watched with expressions they tried to keep neutral.

They made heavier chains. Fenrir wore them. He shook himself. The chains fell off in pieces.

The gods sent to the dwarves — the master craftsmen of Svartalfheim, who could make things that operated on deeper rules than ordinary iron. The dwarves made Gleipnir: a binding that looked like a ribbon of silk, thin and soft and light as a breath of air.

The gods brought Gleipnir to Fenrir.

The wolf looked at the silk ribbon.

He looked at the gods.

"You want me to let you tie me with that?" he said.

"Just as a test of strength," said one of the gods, extremely casually.

Fenrir was not stupid. He looked at the ribbon and thought about how something that looked harmless could still be a trap. He thought about the way the gods were standing, the look on their faces.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Týr and the Binding of Fenrir

"I'll do it," he said slowly, "if one of you puts a hand in my mouth. As a pledge. If this is just a test and you'll let me go, you have nothing to worry about."

The gods looked at each other.

Putting a hand in Fenrir's mouth was not a comfortable proposition under the best circumstances. In the specific circumstance of also tying him with a magical unbreakable ribbon, it was the kind of offer that had "bad ending" written all over it.

Nobody moved.

Tyr stepped forward.

He walked up to Fenrir — the wolf whose head was already the size of a small building — and put his right hand in Fenrir's mouth.

The gods tied the ribbon.

Fenrir pulled. The ribbon tightened. He pulled harder. It tightened more. He thrashed and threw himself against it and the more he fought the more Gleipnir held, the soft silk becoming something that had no give at all.

He looked at Tyr.

Tyr looked back at him.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Týr and the Binding of Fenrir

Tyr had known what was going to happen when he stepped forward. He was the god of justice. He understood that sometimes doing what was necessary cost you something real, and that the cost didn't make the thing less necessary. Asgard needed Fenrir bound. Someone needed to pay for it.

He paid.

He lost his right hand.

He walked back to Asgard one-handed, and he went back to his work — sitting in judgment, keeping the oaths, maintaining the law — and he never complained about it, not once, not in any story that was ever told.

Fenrir howled from where he was bound, a howl that shook the foundations of the world. He would howl until Ragnarok, when everything broke at once.

But he was bound. And Asgard was safer.

Tyr knew the cost. He paid it on purpose.

That's what Tuesday is named after — Tyr's day. It comes around every week, steady and reliable.

Like justice should.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Tyr was the only god who fed Fenrir every day — and that daily act of reliability was what made Fenrir trust him enough to agree to the binding test. What does that tell you about how trust is built? And what do you think it felt like for Tyr when that trust ended the way it did?

Page 16

🎉 Day 149 Complete!

Týr and the Binding of Fenrir

✏️ Grammar & Language: Dialogue Punctuation

When writing dialogue: put spoken words inside quotation marks, use a comma before the closing quote if a dialogue tag follows, capitalize the first word of dialogue, and start a new paragraph for each new speaker.

Example: "I will not yield," said Robin, drawing his bow.
"Then you leave me no choice," the Sheriff replied.

Question 1:

Add correct punctuation to this dialogue:

Where are you going asked the knight I am going to find the truth the hero replied Then I will come with you said his companion

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

The Death of Baldur and Loki's Punishment

The Death of Baldur and Loki's Punishment

A Tale from

The Death of Baldur and Loki's Punishment

A Tale from After Baldur died, everything in Asgard became quieter. Not peaceful-quiet. The other kind.

The gods knew who was responsible. Not Hodr, blind Hodr, who had thrown the dart not knowing what it was — Hodr was grief-stricken and blameless. It was Loki who had found the mistletoe. It was Loki who had guided the hand. It was Loki, in the cave, who had refused to weep.

Loki disappeared.

He had been around long enough to know what was coming, and he was fast enough to get a head start. He built a house in the mountains with four doors facing four directions so he could always see who was approaching. During the days he turned himself into a salmon and hid in a waterfall. At night he sat by the fire and worked on a fishing net, thinking about how the gods might try to catch him.

He looked up one night and saw torches on the distant hills.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

The Death of Baldur and Loki's Punishment

He threw the half-finished net into the fire and jumped into the river.

The gods found the house. They found the ashes in the fire pit. Kvasir — the wisest of all beings, made from the spit of every god in existence — crouched over the ashes and saw the shape of the half-burned net. He understood what it was for. He told the others.

They made their own net from the pattern in the ashes. They went to the waterfall.

Loki, as a salmon, was faster and smarter than the net. He stayed ahead of it when they dragged it forward. He dove to the bottom when they reversed direction. He leaped over it when they swept it back upstream.

He was going to make it.

Then Thor waded to the far end of the river.

The gods dragged the net toward Thor. Loki saw it coming. He bunched his muscles and jumped.

Thor caught him. A salmon. Out of the air. With his bare hands, which were not ordinary hands.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

The Death of Baldur and Loki's Punishment

Loki turned back into himself and looked at the gods standing around him on the riverbank and knew that there was nowhere left to go.

They bound him in a cave beneath the earth. The bonds were made from the entrails of his own son — transformed into iron — and they would not break. They positioned him over two sharp stones. Above him, fixed to the ceiling of the cave, a serpent dripped venom onto his face.

His wife Sigyn was there. Nobody asked her to stay. She didn't leave. She found a bowl and held it above his face, catching the drops. She had been there ever since. She would be there until the end of everything.

When the bowl filled up and she had to step away to empty it, the venom hit Loki's face. The pain of it was enormous. He thrashed against the chains and the earth shook — all of Midgard shaking from the movement of a bound god in a cave beneath it.

That is what earthquakes are, the Norse said. That is what they always were.

The gods knew all of this. Some of them felt something complicated about it — a being who had been their companion, their problem-solver, their impossible wildcard, now under the earth in the dark. A being who had done something unforgivable. Both of these things were true at once, and the Norse sat with both of them without resolving either.

Sigyn was still there with her bowl.

The cave was still dark.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

The Death of Baldur and Loki's Punishment

At Ragnarok, the chains would break. Loki would sail to the final battle on a ship made from the nail clippings of the dead, and he and Heimdall — the two who had always been in opposition, the watcher and the deceiver — would finally face each other and it would be the last thing either of them did.

Not yet.

For now, the earth shook sometimes.

And Sigyn emptied her bowl and came back.

And came back.

And came back.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Loki ran from Asgard knowing what was coming — he even sat by his fire making a fishing net and thinking through how the gods might catch him. What does that preparation tell you about how smart Loki was? And does being smart make what he did better or worse?

Page 15

Definitions

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir

Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir

A Tale from

Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir

A Tale from The sword was called Gram, and it had been broken and reforged, and it was so sharp that when Sigurd tested it in the river it cut a tuft of wool floating on the current clean in two.

He had a dragon to kill.

The dragon's name was Fafnir. He had not always been a dragon. Once he had been a man — the son of a farmer, ordinary except for his strength and his greed. He had killed his own father for a hoard of cursed gold, then transformed himself over years into something that matched the darkness inside him: scales, venom, a terrible size. He lay on the treasure in the wilderness, breathing poison fog, and the curse on the gold kept working on him from within.

The man who had reforged Sigurd's sword was named Regin, and he was Fafnir's brother, which explained the sword and also the problem.

"Kill Fafnir," said Regin. "Bring me the treasure."

Sigurd went to find the path the dragon used between his lair and the river where he drank. He began to dig a pit in the middle of it. An old man wandered up — one eye, wide-brimmed hat, the look of someone who knows far more than he's going to say. He suggested that Sigurd also dig drainage ditches around the main pit, so the blood would flow away and not drown him when it came.

Sigurd dug the pit and the drainage ditches. He climbed in and waited.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir

The ground shook. Fafnir came.

He was very large. He moved the way things move that have forgotten they were ever anything else, heavy and certain and trailing poison in the air around him. He passed over the pit.

Sigurd drove Gram upward with everything he had.

Fafnir stopped.

He looked down at the pit, at the figure in it, at the sword. He lowered his massive head and said, with the dignity of something dying slowly and knowing it: "Who are you? Who sent you?"

Sigurd told him his name.

Fafnir spent his last breaths giving a warning. The gold was cursed. Everyone who touched it died. It had destroyed their father. It had destroyed Fafnir himself. Leave it where it lies.

Sigurd listened politely. Then he took the treasure.

He roasted Fafnir's heart for Regin, as Regin had asked. While he cooked it, he pressed his finger to it to test if it was done, burned himself, and put his finger in his mouth.

Dragon blood.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir

In the trees above him, several birds were having a very clear conversation.

"Regin is going to kill him," one said.

"Obviously," said another. "He's just waiting for the heart."

"He should take the treasure, cut off Regin's head, and ride for the sleeping Valkyrie on the mountain."

The birds went on talking. Sigurd listened.

He made a decision.

He rode on with the treasure — the cursed gold and the ring at its center called Andvaranaut, which a dwarf had once cursed with every ounce of spite he had: everyone who holds this will be destroyed by it.

Sigurd rode through fire to a mountaintop, where a figure lay sleeping in full armor. He cut through the armor and found a woman — a Valkyrie named Brynhild, put to sleep by Odin for defying his will, surrounded by fire that only someone who feared nothing could ride through.

He woke her.

She sat up and looked at him, this young man who had just killed a dragon and ridden through fire to find her, and she saw what he was. He saw what she was.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir

He gave her Andvaranaut as a pledge of his promise to come back.

He had just given her the cursed ring. He didn't know that yet.

He rode on. The curse went with him. The curse worked the way curses do — not through thunder and lightning, but through ordinary human choices, each one understandable, all of them terrible together. A drink that made him forget. A marriage to the wrong person. Brynhild waking up and finding herself somewhere else, with someone else, unable to understand why he had abandoned her.

The end was dark.

The Norse knew it was coming when they started the story. They told it anyway, every word, every tragedy.

Because Sigurd had killed the dragon. He had ridden through the fire. He had woken the sleeper on the mountain.

Those things were real. The curse couldn't take them back.

Some things you do are yours forever, even if everything else falls apart.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

Fafnir warned Sigurd to leave the gold behind — told him directly that the curse was real and everyone who took it would be destroyed. Sigurd took it anyway. Why do you think he didn't listen? Have you ever been warned about something and chosen not to listen? What happened?

Page 15

🎉 Day 152 Complete!

Sigurd and the Dragon Fafnir

Keep reviewing! Quiz on Friday (Day 155).

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

The Mead of Poetry

The Mead of Poetry

A Tale from

The Mead of Poetry

A Tale from In the Norse world, a great poem could make a dead warrior immortal. A terrible poem could kill a man's reputation faster than any sword. The skalds — the Norse poets — were among the most valued people in the land, and a king who had a great skald telling his story would live forever in the telling.

So naturally, there was a magical drink that could turn anyone who tasted it into a genius.

It had been made this way: the two families of gods — the Aesir and the Vanir — had been at war, and when they finally made peace, they sealed the deal by having everyone spit into a bowl. All of them. Every god, every goddess, all into the bowl. From that mingled spit — which sounds disgusting and was — they shaped a being named Kvasir, who was so wise that he could answer any question anyone ever asked.

Kvasir walked through all the worlds sharing his wisdom freely, which was excellent for everyone, until two dwarves named Fjalarr and Galarr killed him, drained his blood into three vessels, and mixed it with honey to make mead.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

The Mead of Poetry

They called it the Mead of Poetry. Anyone who drank it would become a poet or a scholar of the highest order.

The dwarves then made some additional poor decisions involving a giant named Gillingr and his wife, and Gillingr's son Suttungr arrived to take revenge. The dwarves negotiated for their lives by handing over the mead. Suttungr hid it inside a mountain called Hnitbjorg, in a chamber guarded by his daughter Gunnlod, and told everyone the Mead of Poetry was gone forever.

Odin heard about this.

He arrived in the region in disguise — just an old man calling himself Bolverkr, nothing to see here. He found Suttungr's brother Baugi, whose nine thralls had all conveniently died, and offered to do all nine of their jobs in exchange for one drink of the mead. Baugi said the mead wasn't his to give, but he'd ask.

Odin spent the summer doing the work of nine men, which he did without complaint because he was Odin and also because he wanted that mead very badly.

Suttungr said no.

Odin pulled out an auger and asked Baugi to help him drill into the mountain. Baugi felt he owed him something. He drilled. When the hole was through to the chamber inside, Odin turned himself into a snake and slid in.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

The Mead of Poetry

Gunnlod was sitting inside the mountain in the dark, guarding the three vessels of mead. She had been doing this for a very long time. She was not thrilled about her life.

Odin spent three nights with her. He was warm and interesting and he listened, which was more than anyone else had done in a long time. She agreed to let him have three sips of the mead.

Three sips. One per vessel. He drained all three.

Then he turned himself into an eagle and flew.

Suttungr looked up, saw a strange eagle leaving his mountain at speed, grabbed the idea immediately, turned himself into an eagle, and went after him.

Two eagles, one chasing the other, burning across the Norse sky. The gods of Asgard looked up, recognized the eagle in front as Odin somehow, and set out every container they had in the courtyard of Asgard.

Odin hit the walls with Suttungr right behind him. He spat all three vessels' worth of mead into the waiting containers.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

The Mead of Poetry

He made it. Barely. Some mead dribbled out behind him in the chase — that became the portion for bad poets, the verse that almost works but doesn't quite.

Odin shared the Mead of Poetry with the gods and with the human poets who were worthy of it.

He had paid with a summer of hard labor and three nights in a dark mountain. He did not complain about this.

The Norse believed that every great poem — every story told so well it gave you chills, every verse that made someone feel seen, every line so true it stayed in your memory for years — had this in it somewhere. A trace of what Odin traded and carried home in eagle form across the Norse sky.

Next time you read something so good it makes you forget you're reading, that's the mead.

You're welcome, said Odin.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The Mead of Poetry was created from the spit of every god mixed together, which then became a being named Kvasir who knew everything. What do you think the Norse were saying about wisdom with that origin story — where does real wisdom come from, according to this myth?

Page 15

Page 3

✏️ Grammar & Language: Semicolons

A semicolon connects two related independent clauses (complete sentences) that are closely connected in meaning. Use a semicolon instead of a period when the ideas are too connected to separate completely.

Example: The castle was dark; not a single candle burned in any window.

Question 1:

Combine these pairs of sentences using a semicolon:

1. The hero was brave. His enemies feared him. 2. The storm raged all night. By morning, the damage was clear. 3. She had traveled far. Her journey was not yet over.

Page 5

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim

The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim

A Guide to the Norse World After Death

The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim

A Guide to the Norse World After Death

If you were a Norse warrior going into battle, you had a plan. Not just for the battle — for what came after.

If you fought bravely and died on the field, you had a chance of being chosen. Above every battle, invisible to everyone fighting and dying below, the Valkyries rode — armored women on armored horses, watching every warrior, measuring every choice. They were the Choosers of the Slain, and if they found you worthy, they would come for you when you fell.

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim

Worthy meant one specific thing: you had fought without flinching. Not that you'd won. Not that you were the strongest. If you had faced death with full courage and not looked away, you had a chance.

They would carry you to Bifrost, the rainbow bridge, and across it to Valhalla.

Valhalla was Odin's hall, and it was enormous — five hundred and forty doors, each one wide enough for eight hundred warriors walking shoulder to shoulder. The roof was made of shields. The rafters were spears. The walls were lined with the best weapons anyone had ever made.

Inside, the einherjar — the chosen dead — spent every single day doing exactly one thing: fighting. They divided into sides and battled all morning and all afternoon on a great field outside the hall, killing each other thoroughly and efficiently, and then at sunset every wound healed, every dead warrior got back up, and they all went inside for dinner.

Dinner in Valhalla was roasted boar from a great magical pig that was also restored every night, and unlimited mead from a goat on the roof whose udders flowed with it. They ate together, the warriors from every land and every era, people who had been enemies in life now at the same table, all of them training for the same thing.

Ragnarok. The final battle. When that day came, they would march out through the five hundred and forty doors, and there would be a great deal of them.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim

This was not a punishment. This was the honor. The Norse warrior trained his whole life hoping to be found worthy of Valhalla.

But here is the thing: most people didn't die in battle.

Most people died in bed, or at sea, or of sickness, or of old age, in the middle of an ordinary life. These people went to Helheim.

Helheim was large and cold and governed by Hel — daughter of Loki, half living and half dead, a fair ruler in her quiet way. The dead who came to her were not punished. They simply... continued. They ate and drank and talked and existed, but dimly, without the clarity or warmth of life. It was not terrible. It was not glorious. It was simply what came after.

Except for the worst of the dead: oath-breakers, murderers, people who had lived with real dishonor. They went to Nastrond, the Shore of Corpses, the coldest edge of Helheim. The serpent Nidhogg waited for them there. The Norse did not describe it in detail. They didn't need to.

Those who drowned at sea went to Ran, the sea goddess, who caught the drowned in her net and kept them in her hall beneath the waves. This was considered reasonably acceptable, though significantly wetter.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim

What the Norse believed, with all of this, was something important: how you lived and how you died mattered. The universe was paying attention. The Valkyries were watching. The world after death reflected the world before it.

So you trained. You kept your oaths. You faced the hard things without flinching.

Not because life was easy. Because the hard things were coming anyway, and you could face them standing up or you could face them on your knees.

The einherjar in Valhalla, waiting for Ragnarok, had made their choice.

The Norse admired them for it.

The mead was also reportedly excellent.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The Valkyries chose warriors for Valhalla based on courage, not on whether they won. A brave warrior on the losing side might be chosen; a coward on the winning side would not. What does that system say about what the Norse believed really mattered in battle — and in life?

Page 15

🎉 Day 156 Complete!

The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim

What You Accomplished Today:

  • ✅ Learned 3 new vocabulary words
  • ✅ Practiced with word scramble
  • ✅ Read The Viking Afterlife: Valhalla and Helheim
  • ✅ Explored informational text
  • ✅ Answered comprehension questions
  • ✅ Practiced grammar
  • ✅ Completed journal reflection

📚 Week 32 Progress:

🎮 Vocabulary Game

Match each word to its definition! (6 words this week so far)

Score: 0 / 6

Words

valor
domain
eternal
apocalypse
foretold
renewal

Definitions

A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
A vocabulary word from the story
A great catastrophic ending — the destruction of the world as it exists.
Predicted in advance — announced before it happened, often by a prophet or seer.
Starting fresh after something has ended — the world being reborn after destruction.

Page 5

🎯 Vocabulary Practice

Day 4 - Matching Challenge

Page 6

📚 Reading (Part 1 of 4)

Ragnarök: The End and the Beginning

Ragnarok: The End and the Beginning

A Tale from

Ragnarok: The End and the Beginning

A Tale from The Norse knew exactly how the world would end.

They had known for a long time. Odin had gone to Helheim and asked a dead prophetess, and she had told him, and he had come back and not told the other gods everything, and he had spent the years since trying to prepare as much as was preparable.

It wasn't very much.

It would start with Fimbulwinter: three winters in a row, no summer between them. The sky would go dark. The snow would never stop. The cold would get into everything, into the food supply, into the hearts of people, into the bonds that held families and communities together. Old hatreds would come back up. The world would get mean.

Then everything would break at once.

Skoll, the wolf that had been chasing the sun since before you were born, would catch it. Hati would catch the moon. The stars would go out, one by one, until the sky was just black.

Page 7

📚 Reading (Part 2 of 4)

Ragnarök: The End and the Beginning

The earth would shake. Every chain in the nine worlds would snap at the same moment. Loki would climb out of his cave, still angry, his face scarred from the venom. Fenrir would shrug off Gleipnir like it was nothing. The Midgard Serpent would rise from the ocean that had contained him, so enormous that his movement swamped the shore.

Naglfar, the ship made from the nail clippings of the dead, would sail with Loki at the helm and frost giants filling the deck. The sons of Muspel would ride from the south with Surt at their head and his flaming sword burning everything it came near.

Heimdall, at his post, would watch all of this happening.

He would lift Gjallarhorn to his lips. He would blow.

The sound would go through all nine worlds, through every corner of every realm, the signal that had been waiting since the beginning of everything. In Valhalla, all the einherjar — every warrior who had ever been chosen, centuries and centuries of them, training every day for exactly this — would pick up their weapons. Odin would put on his armor. The gods would ride out.

Here is what was foretold, and it happened exactly as foretold:

Odin faced Fenrir. The wolf swallowed him. Odin's son Vidar drove a sword through the wolf's throat in revenge.

Thor killed the Midgard Serpent — finally, after a lifetime of conflict, after everything, the great snake fell. Thor took nine steps and collapsed, overwhelmed by the venom.

Tyr and the great hound Garm killed each other, both falling at the same moment.

Page 8

📚 Reading (Part 3 of 4)

Ragnarök: The End and the Beginning

Freyr faced Surt without his sword. He had given it away for love, years ago. He fought with an antler. He fell.

Loki and Heimdall faced each other at last — the watcher and the deceiver, the loyal and the complicated, the one who had guarded the bridge and the one who had burned it down. They killed each other.

Surt threw fire over the worlds. The seas rose. The earth sank.

And then.

The earth rose again.

New. Green. Covered in grain that nobody had planted. Waterfalls falling in mountain valleys that hadn't existed before. The sky washed clean.

Baldur walked back from Helheim into the sunlight, and his blind brother Hodr was with him, and they stood in the green world and looked at each other.

Odin's sons Vidar and Vali were alive. Thor's sons Modi and Magni were alive, and they had inherited Mjolnir, and when they walked through the ruins of the old Asgard they found, in the grass, the golden game pieces the gods had played with long ago, before everything.

Two humans had sheltered in the branches of Yggdrasil during the fire — a man named Lif and a woman named Lifthrasir. They came down into the green world. They would be the beginning of everything again.

Page 9

📚 Reading (Part 4 of 4)

Ragnarök: The End and the Beginning

This was Ragnarok. The Norse didn't tell this story to make you afraid. They told it to tell you something true.

The world is hard. The cold comes. The bad things happen. The worst things happen. People you love fall.

And then: the earth rises.

A new sun crosses the sky — the daughter of the old one. The grain grows without planting. Baldur is back.

The Norse planted their grain knowing it might fail. They sailed out on seas that might drown them. They fought battles they might not survive. They did all of this not because the world was safe, but because giving up was worse.

Ragnarok was coming. Everyone knew.

Live well anyway.

The earth always rises.

Page 10

Reading Comprehension

Question 1 of 3

Question 1:

The Norse knew exactly how the world would end — who would fight whom, who would fall, who would survive. Odin knew his own death was coming and couldn't stop it. And yet he kept building Valhalla, kept gathering warriors, kept preparing. What does it mean to prepare for something you know you can't win?

Page 16

🎉 Day 157 Complete!

Ragnarök: The End and the Beginning

Day 179 • Week 38
Themes

💭 Common Themes

Many Arabian Nights stories share similar themes:

  • Cleverness defeats power
  • Trust and betrayal
  • Resourcefulness in difficult situations
  • Transformation and growth
  • The importance of thinking before acting

Choose two stories that share a theme. Explain how each story explores that theme differently.

What do you think these stories teach about how to solve problems?

Page 3

Day 180 • Week 38
Language Skills

✏️ Word Roots Explorer

Many English words come from Greek and Latin roots. Understanding these roots helps you figure out new words. Match each root to its meaning, then identify words that use that root.

Question 1

GRAPH (Greek: to write)
Examples: calligraphy, biography, photograph

What does 'calligraphy' mean?

Question 2

MEDI (Latin: middle)
Examples: medieval, Mediterranean, medium

Why is the time period called 'medieval'?

Question 3

VIS/VID (Latin: to see)
Examples: visible, video, vision

If something is 'invisible,' what is it?

Question 4

PORT (Latin: to carry)
Examples: transport, portable, export

What does 'transport' mean?

Page 4

Day 180 • Week 38
Comprehension Review

💭 This Week's Stories

Answer these questions about the stories you read this week:

Question 1

Which story from this week was your favorite? Why?

Question 2

What lesson or theme did you notice appearing in multiple stories this week?

Question 3

Choose one character from this week's stories. What made them memorable?

Page 5

Day 180 • Week 38
Writing

✍️ Reflection

Writing Prompt:

The Arabian Nights stories often feature characters who solve problems through cleverness rather than strength. Write about a time when you (or someone you know) solved a difficult problem by thinking creatively. What was the problem? What creative solution worked?
